items.title: How the dexamethasone suppression test localizes cortisol excess
items.content:
Dexamethasone is a potent synthetic glucocorticoid that suppresses hypothalamic-pituitary CRH/ACTH via negative feedback, lowering endogenous cortisol. The pattern of (non)suppression reveals the cause of Cushing's:
• Autonomous ADRENAL source → ACTH is already suppressed, so dexamethasone changes little (no suppression).
• Pituitary ACTH adenoma (Cushing's disease) → resistant at low dose but usually suppresses at HIGH dose (retains some feedback).
• Ectopic ACTH → typically resistant to suppression at any dose.
So the test both establishes Cushing's and helps differentiate its cause.
keyPoints: Negative feedback is the principle behind the test.
High-dose suppression favors pituitary disease; resistance favors ectopic/adrenal.
Used to diagnose Cushing's AND assist differential diagnosis.
items.reference: Regulatory Control: "If cortisol production is driven by an ACTH-producing pituitary adenoma, dexamethasone suppression is ineffective at lower doses but usually induces suppression at higher doses. If cortisol production is driven by an ectopic source... usually resistant to dexamethasone suppression."

items.title: Tests for adrenal insufficiency: cosyntropin, ITT, metyrapone
items.content:
To assess glucocorticoid DEFICIENCY you stimulate the axis:
• Short cosyntropin (ACTH 1-24) test — 250 µg IM/IV, cortisol at 0/30/60 min; normal = cortisol >15–20 µg/dL (450–550 nmol/L). First-line (safe, reliable).
• Insulin tolerance test (ITT) — insulin-induced hypoglycemia stresses the whole HPA axis (normal cortisol >20 µg/dL, GH >5.1). Gold standard for HPA/GH but CONTRAINDICATED in coronary/cerebrovascular disease and seizure disorders.
• Metyrapone test — blocks 11β-hydroxylase; an intact axis raises 11-deoxycortisol.
keyPoints: Cosyntropin test is first-line for adrenal insufficiency.
ITT is most physiologic but unsafe in cardiac/cerebrovascular/seizure disease.
Never let testing delay treatment in suspected adrenal crisis.
items.reference: Regulatory Control: "The standard ACTH stimulation test involves... cosyntropin (ACTH 1-24), 250 µg IM or IV... A normal response is defined as a cortisol level >15–20 µg/dL... The ITT... is contraindicated in patients with coronary disease, cerebrovascular disease, or seizure disorders."

items.title: Why hypopituitarism spares aldosterone but not cortisol
items.content:
Glucocorticoid (and adrenal androgen) synthesis is driven by pituitary ACTH, but ALDOSTERONE is regulated by the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, not the pituitary. Therefore hypothalamic-pituitary damage impairs cortisol production but leaves aldosterone synthesis intact.

Clinical consequence: SECONDARY (central) adrenal insufficiency causes glucocorticoid deficiency only — patients are not salt-wasting and usually do not need fludrocortisone, and hyperkalemia is not typical. PRIMARY (Addison's) disease loses both, producing hyponatremia AND hyperkalemia.
keyPoints: Aldosterone is RAA-controlled, independent of ACTH.
Secondary AI = glucocorticoid deficiency only (no mineralocorticoid loss).
Primary AI loses both → hyponatremia + hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Regulatory Control: "Because mineralocorticoid synthesis is primarily under the control of the RAA system, hypothalamic-pituitary damage does not significantly impact the capacity of the adrenal to synthesize aldosterone."

items.title: How 11β-HSD2 protects the mineralocorticoid receptor (and SAME/licorice)
items.content:
Cortisol and aldosterone bind the mineralocorticoid receptor (MR) with EQUAL affinity, yet cortisol circulates ~1000× higher. The kidney is protected from cortisol-driven MR activation by 11β-HSD2, which rapidly inactivates cortisol to (inactive) cortisone in mineralocorticoid target tissues.

When this guard fails — inactivating HSD11B2 mutations (syndrome of apparent mineralocorticoid excess, SAME) or 11β-HSD2 inhibition by excess LICORICE — cortisol floods the MR and causes hypokalemic hypertension with SUPPRESSED renin and SUPPRESSED aldosterone. The same can occur when overwhelming cortisol excess (Cushing's) saturates 11β-HSD2.
keyPoints: 11β-HSD2 inactivates cortisol so it can't activate the MR.
SAME / licorice / florid Cushing's → cortisol activates MR.
Result: hypokalemic hypertension with low renin AND low aldosterone.
items.reference: Physiology: "only rapid inactivation of cortisol to cortisone by 11β-HSD2 prevents MR activation by excess cortisol... Inhibition of 11β-HSD2 by excess licorice ingestion also results in hypokalemic hypertension."

items.title: Causes of Cushing's syndrome by frequency
items.content:
• IATROGENIC (exogenous glucocorticoids) is by far the most common cause overall (~1% of people take chronic glucocorticoids).
• Endogenous Cushing's is rare. Of endogenous cases, ~90% are ACTH-DEPENDENT, most commonly a pituitary corticotrope adenoma — 'Cushing's disease' (more common in women). Ectopic ACTH (occult carcinoids of lung/thymus/pancreas; small-cell lung cancer) is more common in men.
• Only ~10% are ACTH-INDEPENDENT (adrenal): cortisol-producing adenoma, adrenocortical carcinoma, or nodular hyperplasia.
keyPoints: Exogenous steroids = most common cause overall.
Endogenous: ~90% ACTH-dependent (Cushing's disease commonest).
Adrenal (ACTH-independent) causes ~10%.
items.reference: Cushing's — Etiology: "Iatrogenic... Cushing's syndrome is by far the most common etiology... Only 10% of patients with Cushing's syndrome have a primary, adrenal cause."

items.title: Why you must confirm Cushing's BEFORE imaging
items.content:
Most management errors (needless surgery/imaging) come from imaging before biochemistry. Pituitary and adrenal 'incidentalomas' are common in the general population, so a scan found before the diagnosis is established can be a red herring.

Correct order: (1) Establish hypercortisolism with screening tests — elevated 24-h urinary free cortisol (×3), failure to suppress morning cortisol after overnight dexamethasone, and loss of diurnal rhythm (high midnight serum/salivary cortisol). (2) Then determine ACTH-dependent vs -independent. (3) ONLY THEN image (adrenal CT for ACTH-independent; pituitary MRI for ACTH-dependent).
keyPoints: Establish the diagnosis biochemically first.
Determine ACTH-dependence before any imaging.
Pituitary/adrenal incidentalomas are common traps.
items.reference: Cushing's — Differential Diagnosis: "imaging should only be used after it is established whether the cortisol excess is ACTH-dependent or ACTH-independent because nodules in the pituitary or the adrenal are a common finding in the general population."

items.title: Why inferior petrosal sinus sampling settles ACTH-dependent Cushing's
items.content:
Pituitary corticotrope adenomas usually retain regulatory features (suppress to high-dose glucocorticoid, respond to CRH/desmopressin); ectopic sources usually do not. But a pituitary MRI is normal in up to 40% (small tumors) and a minority of ectopic tumors mimic pituitary behavior.

When testing is discordant, bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) measures ACTH centrally vs peripherally: a central/peripheral ratio >2 at baseline and >3 after CRH indicates Cushing's DISEASE (pituitary). Caveats: IPSS cannot reliably lateralize the tumor, and no cortisol-lowering drugs should be given beforehand.
keyPoints: Pituitary MRI normal in up to 40% of Cushing's disease.
IPSS ratio >2 baseline / >3 post-CRH = pituitary source.
IPSS does not lateralize; stop cortisol-lowering drugs first.
items.reference: Cushing's — Differential Diagnosis: "An increased central/peripheral plasma ACTH ratio >2 at baseline and >3 at 2–5 min after CRH injection is indicative of Cushing's disease... the results of the IPSS cannot be reliably used for lateralization."

items.title: Which Cushing's features actually discriminate the diagnosis
items.content:
Most features (obesity, diabetes, hypertension, depression, hirsutism) are common in the general population and are NOT discriminating. The features that should raise real suspicion are the catabolic ones: thin fragile skin with easy bruising, broad (>1 cm) PURPLE striae, and proximal MYOPATHY (difficulty rising from a chair/climbing stairs) — plus typical complications appearing at an unusual age (e.g., osteoporosis in a young person).

Ectopic ACTH adds hyperpigmentation (excess ACTH/POMC stimulating melanocytes) and a rapid onset with prominent hypokalemia, edema, and hypertension. Cushing's is also prothrombotic — VTE/PE risk is increased.
keyPoints: Specific clues: purple >1 cm striae, easy bruising, proximal myopathy.
Osteoporosis/diabetes 'at the wrong age' should prompt suspicion.
Hypercoagulable state → DVT/PE risk.
items.reference: Cushing's — Clinical Manifestations: "Distinct features include thinning and fragility of the skin, with easy bruising and broad (>1 cm), purplish stria... and signs of proximal myopathy... can be acutely endangered by deep vein thrombosis."

items.title: Medical control of cortisol excess: drug classes
items.content:
First-line therapy is surgical (transsphenoidal resection for Cushing's disease, cure 60–80%; adrenalectomy for adrenal disease). Medical therapy bridges to surgery or treats unresectable disease:
• 11β-hydroxylase inhibitors: osilodrostat, metyrapone (block cortisol synthesis).
• Steroidogenesis inhibitors: ketoconazole/levoketoconazole.
• GR blocker: mifepristone (blocks cortisol action at tissues).
• Etomidate: potent IV 11β-hydroxylase/aldosterone-synthase blocker for rapid lowering in severe cases.
• Mitotane: adrenolytic, mainly for ACC.
• Cushing's disease specifics: pasireotide (somatostatin agonist), cabergoline (dopamine agonist).
keyPoints: Surgery is first-line; drugs bridge or treat unresectable disease.
Osilodrostat/metyrapone block synthesis; mifepristone blocks the receptor.
Etomidate for rapid control in severe hypercortisolism.
items.reference: Cushing's — Treatment: "Osilodrostat and metyrapone inhibit cortisol synthesis at the level of 11β-hydroxylase... Mifepristone is a GR blocker... etomidate... can be used to rapidly lower cortisol."

items.title: Why glucocorticoid replacement is needed after curing Cushing's
items.content:
Chronic cortisol excess suppresses the HPA axis. After successful removal of an ACTH- or cortisol-producing tumor, the remaining normal axis/adrenal cannot immediately resume cortisol production — the patient is transiently adrenally insufficient. Hence hydrocortisone replacement is started at surgery and slowly tapered as the axis recovers (which can take months to years, and sometimes never fully recovers).

Recovery rates differ by cause: ectopic ACTH ~80% (median 7 months), Cushing's disease ~60% (17 months), adrenal Cushing's ~40% (30 months).
keyPoints: Post-cure patients are transiently adrenally insufficient.
Start hydrocortisone at surgery; taper slowly over months.
Adrenal Cushing's recovers slowest (~40%, ~30 months).
items.reference: Cushing's — Treatment: "After the successful removal of an ACTH- or cortisol-producing tumor, the HPA axis will remain suppressed. Thus, hydrocortisone replacement needs to be initiated at the time of surgery and slowly tapered."

items.title: Why subtype (unilateral vs bilateral) drives aldosteronism treatment
items.content:
Primary aldosteronism is now recognized in 5–12% of hypertensives. The binary classification matters because it dictates therapy:
• UNILATERAL aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn's) → potentially CURATIVE laparoscopic adrenalectomy.
• BILATERAL adrenal hyperplasia (the most common form) → medical therapy with a mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone).

This is why subtyping (CT then adrenal vein sampling in surgical candidates) is essential before deciding between surgery and lifelong MR antagonism.
keyPoints: Unilateral adenoma → surgery (curative).
Bilateral hyperplasia (commonest) → MR antagonist.
Subtyping changes management, hence AVS in surgical candidates.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Etiology: "unilateral forms are amenable to potentially curative surgery, whereas mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist therapy is the treatment of choice for bilateral forms."

items.title: Who to screen for primary aldosteronism, and how
items.content:
Don't screen every hypertensive — target those with: drug-resistant hypertension (>3 drugs), hypokalemia (spontaneous or diuretic-induced), an adrenal mass, onset before age 40, or a family history of primary aldosteronism/early stroke.

Screening test = aldosterone-renin ratio (ARR). Practical points: NORMALIZE potassium first (hypokalemia suppresses aldosterone, causing false negatives); stop MR antagonists 4–6 weeks before; beta-blockers cause false positives and ACE/ARB cause false negatives. A positive ARR is then confirmed (e.g., saline infusion test — aldosterone fails to suppress).
keyPoints: Screen resistant HTN, hypokalemia, adrenal mass, onset <40, family history.
ARR is the screen; correct potassium and stop MR antagonists first.
Confirm with saline infusion / sodium loading / fludrocortisone suppression.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Diagnosis: "screening... should be restricted to those who exhibit hypertension associated with drug resistance, hypokalemia, an adrenal mass, onset of disease before the age of 40 years, or family history... serum potassium needs to be normalized prior to testing."

items.title: Why most patients with primary aldosteronism are NOT hypokalemic
items.content:
The textbook hallmark is hypokalemic hypertension, but only 10–40% of patients with primary aldosteronism are actually hypokalemic. Aldosterone drives renal sodium retention and potassium/hydrogen excretion (via ENaC), producing volume expansion, hypertension, and metabolic alkalosis — but serum sodium tends to stay NORMAL because water is retained alongside sodium.

Clinical lesson: a normal potassium does NOT exclude primary aldosteronism — which is why resistant hypertension alone (even normokalemic) is a screening indication.
keyPoints: Only 10–40% have hypokalemia — normal K+ doesn't exclude it.
Sodium is usually normal (concurrent water retention).
Expect metabolic alkalosis; screen resistant HTN regardless of K+.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Clinical: "only 10–40% of patients with primary aldosteronism exhibit hypokalemia. Serum sodium tends to be normal due to the concurrent fluid retention."

items.title: When to do adrenal vein sampling — and why CT alone isn't enough
items.content:
After biochemical confirmation, fine-cut adrenal CT is done, but a small adenoma may be missed (<5 mm) and a non-functioning incidentaloma may masquerade as the culprit. So adrenal vein sampling (AVS) — comparing aldosterone (corrected for cortisol) between the two adrenal veins — is used in SURGICAL CANDIDATES who either have no clear lesion or are >35 years with a unilateral lesion (because older patients more often have coincidental adenomas).

Exception: a patient <35 with a clear unilateral lesion and no familial suspicion can go straight to surgery. [11C]metomidate PET-CT is a validated non-invasive alternative to AVS.
keyPoints: CT can miss small adenomas / mislabel incidentalomas.
AVS for surgical candidates, especially >35 with a unilateral lesion.
<35 with clear unilateral lesion → straight to surgery.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Treatment: "AVS should only be carried out in surgical candidates with either no obvious lesion on CT or evidence of a unilateral lesion but with age >35 years because the latter patients have a higher likelihood of harboring a coincidental, endocrine-inactive adrenal adenoma."

items.title: GRA and Liddle's: mineralocorticoid hypertension that defies the usual rules
items.content:
• Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism (GRA): a chimeric CYP11B1/CYP11B2 gene puts aldosterone synthase under ACTH control, so aldosterone rises with ACTH and is SUPPRESSED BY DEXAMETHASONE. Suspect with early-onset hypertension/strokes and a dominant family history; hybrid steroids (18-oxo/18-hydroxycortisol) are elevated. Treat with low-dose dexamethasone (± MR antagonist).
• Liddle's syndrome: constitutively active ENaC (β/γ subunit mutations) causes hypokalemic hypertension with LOW renin and LOW aldosterone. It responds to AMILORIDE (ENaC blocker) but NOT to MR antagonists (the receptor isn't the problem).
keyPoints: GRA: aldosterone is ACTH-driven → dexamethasone-responsive.
Liddle's: ENaC overactivity → amiloride works, spironolactone doesn't.
Both can present as young-onset, low-renin hypertension.
items.reference: Aldosteronism: "glucocorticoid suppression can reduce aldosterone production" (GRA); Liddle's: "very sensitive to amiloride treatment but will not respond to MR antagonist treatment because the defect is due to a constitutively active ENaC."

items.title: The two questions every adrenal incidentaloma must answer
items.content:
Adrenal incidentalomas are common (2–5% of adults; rises with age). Every mass >1 cm needs to answer: (1) Does it autonomously secrete hormone that could harm health? and (2) Is it benign or malignant?

Hormonal workup: MANDATORY exclusion of pheochromocytoma (lipid-poor masses); screen for primary aldosteronism if hypertensive; screen for cortisol autonomy (1-mg overnight dexamethasone — MACS if cortisol >50 nmol/L without overt Cushing's). DHEA/precursor excess suggests ACC.
keyPoints: Ask: hormonally active? and benign vs malignant?
Always exclude pheochromocytoma; screen aldosteronism if hypertensive.
Overnight 1-mg dexamethasone screens for autonomous cortisol (MACS).
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "Two key questions need to be addressed: (1) Does the tumor autonomously secrete hormones... (2) Is the adrenal mass benign or malignant? ... Exclusion of catecholamine excess from a pheochromocytoma... is a mandatory part of the diagnostic workup."

items.title: How CT attenuation (Hounsfield units) and size triage adrenal masses
items.content:
On UNENHANCED CT, lipid-rich adenomas have low attenuation; ACC and pheochromocytoma have high attenuation (>20 HU).
• <10 HU at any size with normal biochemistry → benign (malignancy <0.5%), no follow-up.
• <4 cm and 10–20 HU → also benign.
• <4 cm + >20 HU, or >4 cm + 10–20 HU → low risk (<5%), use interval imaging.
• >4 cm AND >20 HU → ~50% malignancy risk → swift surgery/evaluation.
Urine steroid metabolomics has twice the positive predictive value of imaging for detecting ACC.
keyPoints: <10 HU (any size) = benign, no follow-up.
>4 cm + >20 HU = ~50% malignancy → act swiftly.
Attenuation is meaningful only on UNenhanced CT.
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "Adrenal masses of any size with normal endocrine biochemistry... and an attenuation value of <10 HU on unenhanced CT can be considered benign (malignancy rate <0.5%)... masses >4 cm with an attenuation >20 HU... with a 50% risk of underlying malignancy."

items.title: Why adrenal biopsy/FNA is rarely done
items.content:
FNA of an adrenal mass is dangerous and usually unhelpful:
• FNA of a PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA can trigger a life-threatening hypertensive crisis — so pheochromocytoma must be excluded first.
• FNA of an ACC violates the tumor capsule and can cause needle-track metastasis, and it cannot reliably distinguish benign from malignant adrenocortical lesions.

FNA is reserved for a patient with a KNOWN non-adrenal cancer and a new adrenal mass (to confirm metastasis), only after excluding pheochromocytoma and if the result will change management — remembering that ~25% of such masses are NOT metastases.
keyPoints: Pheo FNA → hypertensive crisis; ACC FNA → capsule violation/seeding.
FNA can't tell benign from malignant adrenocortical tumors.
Only for suspected metastasis after excluding pheo.
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "FNA of a pheochromocytoma can cause a life-threatening hypertensive crisis. FNA of an ACC violates the tumor capsule and can cause needle track metastasis."

items.title: Adrenocortical carcinoma: diagnosis, prognosis, and mitotane
items.content:
ACC is rare (1–2/million) and aggressive. Key genetics: TP53 (25% sporadic; Li-Fraumeni germline; R337H in Brazilian pediatric cases) and IGF2 overexpression (90%). FNA is contraindicated (can't distinguish benign/malignant; capsule violation). The Weiss score (≥3 elements) diagnoses ACC, but Ki67 is the key PROGNOSTIC marker (Ki67 ≥10% = poor prognosis).

Cure requires early, complete surgical resection by a specialist. Adjuvant MITOTANE (adrenolytic) is given for high-risk disease (stage III/IV, Ki67 ≥10%) for ≥2 years. Because mitotane disrupts cortisol synthesis AND induces CYP3A4 (rapidly inactivating glucocorticoids) and raises CBG, glucocorticoid replacement must be roughly DOUBLE the usual dose.
keyPoints: Ki67 ≥10% = poor prognosis; Weiss score diagnoses ACC.
Cure = complete specialist surgery; adjuvant mitotane for high risk.
Mitotane needs ~double glucocorticoid replacement (CYP3A4 induction).
items.reference: ACC: "the most important prognostic histopathologic parameter is the Ki67 proliferation index... glucocorticoid replacement dose should be at least double... because mitotane induces hepatic CYP3A4 activity, resulting in rapid inactivation of glucocorticoids."

items.title: Why primary adrenal insufficiency causes hyperpigmentation
items.content:
In primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's), low cortisol removes negative feedback, so the pituitary makes excess POMC, which is cleaved to ACTH AND to melanocyte-stimulating peptides. High ACTH/POMC products stimulate melanocytes → HYPERPIGMENTATION, most marked at friction areas, scars, and sun-exposed skin.

Contrast: SECONDARY (central) adrenal insufficiency has LOW ACTH, so the skin is pale ('alabaster'). Hyperpigmentation is thus a clinical clue that the lesion is in the adrenal (primary), not the pituitary.
keyPoints: High ACTH/POMC → melanocyte stimulation → hyperpigmentation (primary).
Secondary AI = low ACTH → pallor.
Pigmentation localizes the lesion to the adrenal.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Clinical: "A distinguishing feature of primary adrenal insufficiency is hyperpigmentation, which is caused by excess ACTH stimulation of melanocytes... in secondary adrenal insufficiency, the skin has an alabaster-like paleness."

items.title: Causes of Addison's disease and the polyglandular syndromes
items.content:
Autoimmune adrenalitis is the most common cause of primary adrenal insufficiency (isolated in 30–40%; part of autoimmune polyglandular syndromes in 60–70%):
• APS1 (APECED): autosomal recessive, AIRE gene — chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis + hypoparathyroidism + Addison's.
• APS2: polygenic, HLA-DR3 — autoimmune thyroid disease + vitiligo + premature ovarian failure (± type 1 diabetes, pernicious anemia).
In males with negative adrenal antibodies, check very-long-chain fatty acids to exclude X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (ABCD1). TB adrenalitis remains common in developing countries.
keyPoints: Autoimmune adrenalitis is the leading cause; screen steroid autoantibodies.
APS1 = AIRE + candidiasis + hypoparathyroidism; APS2 = HLA-DR3 + thyroid/vitiligo.
Antibody-negative males: check VLCFA for adrenoleukodystrophy.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Etiology: "Primary adrenal insufficiency is most commonly caused by autoimmune adrenalitis... APS1... is caused by mutations in the autoimmune regulator gene AIRE... In male patients with negative autoantibodies... very-long-chain fatty acids should be measured to exclude X-ALD."

items.title: How to recognize and treat adrenal crisis
items.content:
Adrenal crisis (acute adrenal insufficiency) is more common in PRIMARY disease (loss of both cortisol and aldosterone). It presents with postural hypotension progressing to hypovolemic shock, and may MIMIC an acute abdomen (pain, nausea, vomiting, fever) or neurologic decline. It is precipitated by intercurrent illness, surgery/stress, or increased cortisol clearance (e.g., hyperthyroidism).

Treatment cannot wait for confirmatory tests: draw a baseline cortisol, then immediately give IV saline (≈1 L/h with cardiac monitoring) and hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus followed by 200 mg/24 h. Mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) is added only once the hydrocortisone dose falls <50 mg/day (high-dose hydrocortisone itself stimulates the MR).
keyPoints: Can mimic acute abdomen; suspect in shock + hyponatremia/hyperkalemia.
Treat first: saline 1 L/h + hydrocortisone 100 mg then 200 mg/24 h.
Don't delay treatment for testing.
items.reference: Treatment — Acute Adrenal Insufficiency: "immediate initiation of rehydration... saline infusion at initial rates of 1 L/h... Glucocorticoid replacement should be initiated by bolus injection of 100 mg hydrocortisone, followed by... 200 mg hydrocortisone over 24 h."

items.title: Chronic replacement and stress dosing in Addison's
items.content:
Glucocorticoid: hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day in 2–3 divided doses, with at least half on waking to mimic the morning cortisol peak. Long-acting steroids (prednisolone/dexamethasone) are avoided routinely (excess exposure at physiologic nadir).

Mineralocorticoid (primary AI only): fludrocortisone 100–150 µg, titrated to BP, electrolytes, and renin (keep renin upper-normal); increase in hot weather. STRESS DOSING is critical: double the oral dose for febrile illness; give 100 mg IM/IV hydrocortisone then 200 mg/24 h for vomiting, surgery, or trauma. Every patient needs an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit and a steroid alert card/bracelet.
keyPoints: Hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day, most in the morning.
Fludrocortisone (primary AI); monitor renin (upper normal).
Stress dose: double for illness; 100 mg IM/IV for vomiting/surgery/trauma.
items.reference: Treatment: "15–25 mg hydrocortisone in two to three divided doses... at least one-half of the daily dose should be administered in the morning... Mineralocorticoid replacement... 100–150 µg fludrocortisone... doubling the routine oral glucocorticoid dose in the case of intercurrent illness."

items.title: Why and how to taper long-term glucocorticoids
items.content:
Exogenous glucocorticoids suppress the HPA axis. Supraphysiologic doses for >3–4 weeks carry the highest risk of glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency (GI-AI); courses <3–4 weeks can be stopped abruptly. CYP3A4 inhibitors (notably ritonavir, plus azoles, some antibiotics) and opioids markedly increase risk.

Taper gradually to a physiologic dose (~3–5 mg prednisone), then test HPA recovery with an early-morning cortisol: <150 nmol/L (5 µg/dL) suggests GI-AI; >300 nmol/L (10 µg/dL) suggests recovery (indeterminate → cosyntropin test). Until recovery, patients need stress-dose coverage; mineralocorticoid replacement is NOT needed (aldosterone is preserved).
keyPoints: >3–4 weeks supraphysiologic = highest GI-AI risk; <3–4 weeks can stop abruptly.
Taper to ~physiologic dose, then check morning cortisol (5 vs 10 µg/dL).
No mineralocorticoid needed; still give stress doses until recovery.
items.reference: GI-AI: "patients treated with supraphysiologic doses for longer than 3–4 weeks carry the highest risk, whereas short-term therapy is generally safe... an early-morning cortisol <150 nmol/L (or 5 μg/dL) is highly suggestive of GI-AI, whereas values >300 nmol/L (or 10 μg/dL) are in keeping with HPA axis recovery."

items.title: How 21-hydroxylase deficiency produces deficiency AND androgen excess
items.content:
21-Hydroxylase (CYP21A2) deficiency causes 90–95% of CAH. The enzyme block impairs cortisol (and, if severe, aldosterone) synthesis. Low cortisol removes negative feedback → ACTH rises → it drives the adrenal, but because the pathway to cortisol is blocked, precursors are SHUNTED into androgen synthesis → androgen EXCESS.

Severity spectrum: classic salt-wasting (combined gluco/mineralocorticoid deficiency, neonatal salt-wasting crisis) → simple virilizing (glucocorticoid deficiency only) → nonclassic (mild; presents in adolescence/adulthood with a PCOS-like picture, preserved cortisol). 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17OHP) accumulates and is the diagnostic marker.
keyPoints: Block → low cortisol → high ACTH → precursors shunted to androgens.
Spectrum: salt-wasting → simple virilizing → nonclassic (PCOS-like).
17OHP is the diagnostic (and overtreatment) marker.
items.reference: CAH: "21-Hydroxylase deficiency disrupts glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid synthesis... This leads to increased pituitary ACTH release, which drives increased synthesis of adrenal androgen precursors and subsequent androgen excess."

items.title: Why CAH treatment is harder than ordinary adrenal replacement
items.content:
In CAH, glucocorticoid serves TWO purposes: replace deficient cortisol AND suppress the excess ACTH drive that causes androgen excess. Because current steroids don't mimic the diurnal cortisol curve, early-morning ACTH escape drives androgen production — so dosing is a balance between under-suppression (androgen excess) and over-treatment (Cushingoid side effects, growth suppression in children, osteoporosis/metabolic syndrome in adults).

Monitor androstenedione/testosterone (target sex-specific normal); 17OHP within the normal control range signals OVER-treatment. Men may develop testicular adrenal rest tumors (TART) that impair fertility. Stress dosing and (in primary forms) mineralocorticoid replacement still apply.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoid both replaces cortisol and suppresses androgen drive.
Monitor androstenedione/testosterone; normal 17OHP = overtreatment.
Watch for testicular adrenal rest tumors (TART) in men.
items.reference: CAH — Treatment: "Glucocorticoid treatment is more complex... it not only needed to replace missing glucocorticoids but also to control the increased ACTH drive and subsequent androgen excess... 17OHP is a useful marker of overtreatment."

items.title: Why diagnosis relies on stimulation, not random cortisol
items.content:
A single random cortisol is unreliable because cortisol has a strong diurnal rhythm — a low value may simply reflect timing. Many patients with secondary adrenal insufficiency even have near-normal basal cortisol but fail to mount a response when challenged. The short cosyntropin test (cortisol <450–500 nmol/L at 30–60 min = fail) reveals this reserve deficit.

After confirming insufficiency, plasma ACTH separates primary (HIGH ACTH) from secondary (low/inappropriately normal ACTH); high renin confirms mineralocorticoid deficiency in primary disease.
keyPoints: Random cortisol is unreliable (diurnal variation).
Cosyntropin test exposes inadequate cortisol reserve.
ACTH then localizes: high = primary, low = secondary.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Diagnosis: "Random serum cortisol measurements are of limited diagnostic value because baseline cortisol levels may be coincidentally low... measurement of plasma ACTH is the next step, with increased or inappropriately low levels defining primary and secondary origin."

items.title: Why hyponatremia can occur even in SECONDARY adrenal insufficiency
items.content: In primary AI, hyponatremia (≈80% at presentation) and hyperkalemia (≈40%) result mainly from mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) deficiency. But hyponatremia can also occur in SECONDARY AI, where aldosterone is intact: cortisol normally inhibits ADH (vasopressin) release, so cortisol deficiency disinhibits ADH → a mild SIADH-like picture with water retention and hyponatremia (without hyperkalemia, since aldosterone is preserved).
keyPoints: Primary: hyponatremia + hyperkalemia (aldosterone loss).
Secondary: hyponatremia possible (cortisol loss → ↑ADH), but no hyperkalemia.
Glucocorticoid deficiency also transiently raises TSH.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Clinical: "Hyponatremia... can also occur in secondary adrenal insufficiency due to diminished inhibition of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) release by cortisol, resulting in mild syndrome of inappropriate secretion of antidiuretic hormone (SIADH)."

items.question: A patient has clinical features suggesting Cushing's syndrome and an incidentally noted small pituitary lesion on a prior MRI. What is the correct FIRST step?
options: Biochemically establish hypercortisolism before any further imaging or surgery
Order a high-dose dexamethasone suppression test to localize
items.explanation: Cushing's must be confirmed biochemically (24-h urinary free cortisol ×3, overnight dexamethasone suppression, loss of diurnal rhythm) BEFORE imaging or surgery, because pituitary/adrenal incidentalomas are common in the general population and acting on imaging first leads to unnecessary procedures.
items.reference: Cushing's — Diagnosis: "establishing the diagnosis of Cushing's beyond doubt prior to employing any tests used for the differential diagnosis... imaging should only be used after it is established whether the cortisol excess is ACTH-dependent or ACTH-independent."

items.question: In a patient with confirmed Cushing's syndrome, a SUPPRESSED plasma ACTH indicates which type?
items.explanation: A suppressed ACTH means cortisol production is autonomous (adrenal), suppressing pituitary ACTH by negative feedback → ACTH-independent disease, prompting adrenal CT. Normal/high ACTH indicates ACTH-dependent disease (pituitary or ectopic), prompting pituitary MRI and further differentiation.
items.reference: Cushing's — Differential Diagnosis: "plasma ACTH levels are suppressed in cases of autonomous adrenal cortisol excess... patients with ACTH-dependent Cushing's have normal or increased plasma ACTH."

items.question: A patient with ACTH-dependent Cushing's has a normal pituitary MRI. Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling shows a central:peripheral ACTH ratio of 4 after CRH. What does this indicate?
items.explanation: A central/peripheral ACTH ratio >2 at baseline or >3 after CRH indicates a pituitary source (Cushing's disease). IPSS is especially useful when the MRI is normal (up to 40% of cases) or testing is discordant. Note IPSS cannot reliably lateralize the tumor.
items.reference: Cushing's — Differential Diagnosis: "An increased central/peripheral plasma ACTH ratio >2 at baseline and >3 at 2–5 min after CRH injection is indicative of Cushing's disease."

items.question: Which finding is MOST discriminating (specific) for Cushing's syndrome rather than common comorbidity?
options: Broad (>1 cm) purple striae with easy bruising and proximal myopathy
items.explanation: Obesity, diabetes, and hypertension are common and non-specific. The catabolic features — thin skin with easy bruising, broad purple striae (>1 cm), and proximal myopathy — are far more discriminating, as is a typical complication occurring at an unusual age (e.g., osteoporosis in a young person).
items.reference: Cushing's — Clinical: "Distinct features include thinning and fragility of the skin, with easy bruising and broad (>1 cm), purplish stria... and signs of proximal myopathy."

items.question: Which acute, potentially life-threatening complication is patients with Cushing's syndrome particularly prone to?
items.explanation: Cortisol excess creates a hypercoagulable state, so patients are at risk of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — relevant to perioperative risk, which medical pretreatment can help reduce. Cushing's causes hyperglycemia, not hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Cushing's — Clinical: "Patients with Cushing's syndrome can be acutely endangered by deep vein thrombosis, with subsequent pulmonary embolism, due to a hypercoagulable state."

items.question: Which drug controls hypercortisolism by BLOCKING the glucocorticoid receptor rather than inhibiting cortisol synthesis?
items.explanation: Mifepristone is a glucocorticoid-receptor antagonist, mitigating cortisol's effects at target tissues. Metyrapone and osilodrostat inhibit 11β-hydroxylase, and ketoconazole inhibits early steroidogenesis — all reduce cortisol synthesis rather than block its receptor.
items.reference: Cushing's — Treatment: "Mifepristone is a GR blocker, hence mitigating the effects of cortisol excess on target tissues."

items.question: Immediately after successful transsphenoidal removal of an ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma, what is essential management?
options: No replacement is needed because cortisol was high
Hydrocortisone replacement started at surgery and slowly tapered as the suppressed HPA axis recovers
items.explanation: Chronic cortisol excess suppresses the HPA axis, so after cure the patient is transiently adrenally insufficient. Hydrocortisone is started at surgery and slowly tapered as the axis recovers (months to years). Mineralocorticoid replacement is not needed (aldosterone is RAA-controlled).
items.reference: Cushing's — Treatment: "hydrocortisone replacement needs to be initiated at the time of surgery and slowly tapered following recovery."

items.question: Which patient with hypertension should be screened for primary aldosteronism?
options: A 60-year-old with well-controlled hypertension on one drug
A 45-year-old with hypertension resistant to three drugs and spontaneous hypokalemia
A 70-year-old with isolated systolic hypertension and normal potassium
items.explanation: Screening is targeted to drug-resistant hypertension, hypokalemia, adrenal mass, onset before 40, or a family history of primary aldosteronism/early stroke. Resistant hypertension with hypokalemia is a classic indication. Routine, easily controlled hypertension is not screened.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Diagnosis: "screening... should be restricted to those who exhibit hypertension associated with drug resistance, hypokalemia, an adrenal mass, onset of disease before the age of 40 years, or family history of primary aldosteronism."

items.question: Before measuring the aldosterone-renin ratio (ARR) to screen for primary aldosteronism, which step is essential?
options: Load the patient with potassium to raise it above normal
Normalize serum potassium and stop mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonists 4–6 weeks beforehand
items.explanation: Potassium must be normalized before testing (hypokalemia suppresses aldosterone, causing false negatives), and MR antagonists must be stopped 4–6 weeks beforehand. Beta-blockers cause false positives; ACE/ARBs cause false negatives. The saline infusion test is a CONFIRMATORY test, done after a positive ARR.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Diagnosis: "serum potassium needs to be normalized prior to testing... MR antagonists need to be ceased at least 4–6 weeks prior to ARR measurement."

items.question: Adrenal vein sampling confirms a unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma in a 38-year-old. What is the preferred treatment?
items.explanation: A lateralized unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma is treated with laparoscopic adrenalectomy, which can be curative. Bilateral hyperplasia is treated medically with an MR antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone). Dexamethasone is for GRA; amiloride for Liddle's.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Treatment: "unilateral forms are amenable to potentially curative surgery... Laparoscopic adrenalectomy is the preferred approach."

items.question: A patient with resistant hypertension and a NORMAL serum potassium is being evaluated. Which statement is correct regarding primary aldosteronism?
options: Only 10–40% of patients with primary aldosteronism are hypokalemic, so normal potassium does not exclude it
Hypokalemia is present in all cases
items.explanation: Only 10–40% of patients with primary aldosteronism have hypokalemia, so normokalemia does not exclude the diagnosis — resistant hypertension alone is a screening indication. Serum sodium is usually normal because of concurrent water retention.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Clinical: "only 10–40% of patients with primary aldosteronism exhibit hypokalemia."

items.question: A young patient has hypokalemic hypertension with LOW renin and LOW aldosterone; a normal urinary steroid profile; and the hypertension responds to amiloride but NOT spironolactone. What is the diagnosis?
items.explanation: Liddle's syndrome is caused by a constitutively active epithelial sodium channel (ENaC). It responds to amiloride (an ENaC blocker) but not to MR antagonists because the receptor is not the problem. Renin and aldosterone are both suppressed.
items.reference: Aldosteronism: Liddle's syndrome "is very sensitive to amiloride treatment but will not respond to MR antagonist treatment because the defect is due to a constitutively active ENaC."

items.question: A family has dominantly inherited early-onset hypertension and strokes; aldosterone is high but suppresses with dexamethasone, and hybrid steroids (18-oxocortisol) are elevated. What is the condition, and its treatment?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism (GRA) results from a chimeric CYP11B1/CYP11B2 gene placing aldosterone synthase under ACTH control; aldosterone is therefore suppressible by dexamethasone, and hybrid steroids (18-oxo/18-hydroxycortisol) are elevated. Treatment is low-dose dexamethasone (± MR antagonist).
items.reference: Aldosteronism: GRA "is caused by a chimeric gene... aldosterone production is regulated by ACTH rather than by renin... glucocorticoid suppression can reduce aldosterone production."

items.question: A child has severe hypokalemic hypertension with LOW renin and LOW aldosterone, and an increased free-cortisol-to-cortisone ratio. Excess ingestion of which substance can produce an identical picture?
items.explanation: This is the syndrome of apparent mineralocorticoid excess (SAME) — 11β-HSD2 deficiency lets cortisol activate the mineralocorticoid receptor. Excess licorice inhibits 11β-HSD2 and reproduces the same hypokalemic hypertension with suppressed renin and aldosterone.
items.reference: Physiology/Aldosteronism: "the syndrome of apparent mineralocorticoid excess (SAME)... Inhibition of 11β-HSD2 by excess licorice ingestion also results in hypokalemic hypertension."

items.question: An incidental 2.5 cm adrenal mass with normal endocrine screening has an attenuation of 6 HU on unenhanced CT. What is the appropriate management?
options: Consider it benign (malignancy <0.5%); no further imaging follow-up needed
items.explanation: An adrenal mass with <10 HU on unenhanced CT and normal hormonal workup is lipid-rich and benign (malignancy rate <0.5%), requiring no further imaging follow-up. High-risk masses are those >4 cm with >20 HU.
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "Adrenal masses of any size with normal endocrine biochemistry... and an attenuation value of <10 HU on unenhanced CT can be considered benign (malignancy rate <0.5%) and do not require further follow-up."

items.question: What is a MANDATORY part of the hormonal workup of any lipid-poor adrenal incidentaloma before considering biopsy or surgery?
items.explanation: Pheochromocytoma must be excluded in the workup of a lipid-poor adrenal mass because an unrecognized pheochromocytoma can cause a life-threatening hypertensive crisis (e.g., during biopsy or surgery). Aldosteronism should also be excluded if hypertensive, and cortisol autonomy screened.
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "Exclusion of catecholamine excess from a pheochromocytoma arising from the adrenal medulla is a mandatory part of the diagnostic workup of lipid-poor masses."

items.question: Why is FNA/biopsy of an adrenal mass generally avoided?
options: It can trigger a hypertensive crisis if the mass is a pheochromocytoma, and can seed tumor (needle-track metastasis) if it is an adrenocortical carcinoma
items.explanation: Biopsy of a pheochromocytoma can precipitate a life-threatening hypertensive crisis, and biopsy of an ACC violates the capsule and can cause needle-track metastasis. Moreover, FNA cannot distinguish benign from malignant adrenocortical lesions. It is reserved for suspected metastasis after excluding pheochromocytoma.
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "FNA of a pheochromocytoma can cause a life-threatening hypertensive crisis. FNA of an ACC violates the tumor capsule and can cause needle track metastasis."

items.question: Which histopathologic parameter is the most important PROGNOSTIC marker in adrenocortical carcinoma?
items.explanation: The Ki67 proliferation index is the most important prognostic marker (Ki67 ≥10% indicates poor prognosis with high recurrence/rapid progression). The Weiss score is used to DIAGNOSE ACC (≥3 elements) but carries no prognostic value.
items.reference: ACC: "the most important prognostic histopathologic parameter is the Ki67 proliferation index... a Ki67 ≥10% is associated with poor prognosis."

items.question: A patient on adjuvant mitotane after resection of adrenocortical carcinoma requires glucocorticoid replacement. How should the dose compare to standard adrenal insufficiency replacement?
options: About double the usual dose, because mitotane induces CYP3A4 and raises CBG
items.explanation: Mitotane disrupts cortisol synthesis AND induces hepatic CYP3A4 (rapidly inactivating glucocorticoids) and raises cortisol-binding globulin (lowering free cortisol). Therefore glucocorticoid replacement must be roughly double the usual adrenal-insufficiency dose.
items.reference: ACC: "glucocorticoid replacement dose should be at least double of that usually used in adrenal insufficiency... because mitotane induces hepatic CYP3A4 activity."

items.question: A patient with chronic adrenal insufficiency has hyperpigmentation of the knuckles, scars, and buccal mucosa. What does this indicate?
options: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's) with elevated ACTH
items.explanation: Hyperpigmentation results from excess ACTH/POMC products stimulating melanocytes, which occurs in PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol → high ACTH). Secondary adrenal insufficiency has low ACTH and pale skin.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Clinical: "A distinguishing feature of primary adrenal insufficiency is hyperpigmentation, which is caused by excess ACTH stimulation of melanocytes."

items.question: Which electrolyte pattern is characteristic of PRIMARY (but not typically secondary) adrenal insufficiency?
items.explanation: Primary adrenal insufficiency loses aldosterone, producing hyponatremia (~80%) AND hyperkalemia (~40%). Secondary AI (aldosterone preserved) can have hyponatremia (cortisol deficiency → increased ADH) but NOT hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Clinical: "Hyponatremia is a characteristic biochemical feature in primary adrenal insufficiency and is found in 80% of patients... Hyperkalemia is present in 40% of patients."

items.question: A patient presents in shock with hypotension, vomiting, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia; adrenal crisis is suspected. What is the correct immediate management?
options: Withhold steroids until the cosyntropin test result returns
Draw a baseline cortisol, then immediately give IV saline and hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus, then 200 mg/24 h)
Give oral hydrocortisone 20 mg and discharge
items.explanation: Treatment must not wait for testing: draw a baseline cortisol, then give IV saline (~1 L/h) and hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus followed by 200 mg/24 h. Fludrocortisone is added only once hydrocortisone falls below ~50 mg/day (high-dose hydrocortisone itself stimulates the MR).
items.reference: Treatment — Acute Adrenal Insufficiency: "Glucocorticoid replacement should be initiated by bolus injection of 100 mg hydrocortisone, followed by... 200 mg hydrocortisone over 24 h... tests to establish the diagnosis... should never delay treatment."

items.question: Which is the preferred glucocorticoid regimen for chronic replacement in adrenal insufficiency?
options: Hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day in 2–3 divided doses, with at least half on waking
items.explanation: Physiologic replacement uses hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day in 2–3 divided doses, with at least half given in the morning to mimic the cortisol peak. Long-acting steroids (dexamethasone/prednisolone) are avoided routinely because they over-expose tissues during the physiologic nadir.
items.reference: Treatment: "15–25 mg hydrocortisone in two to three divided doses... at least one-half of the daily dose should be administered in the morning... Long-acting glucocorticoids such as prednisolone or dexamethasone are not preferred."

items.question: A patient with Addison's disease is undergoing major surgery. What glucocorticoid coverage is needed?
options: Stress dosing: 100 mg IM/IV hydrocortisone followed by 200 mg/24 h
items.explanation: Major stress (surgery, trauma, prolonged vomiting) requires parenteral stress-dose hydrocortisone — 100 mg IM/IV followed by 200 mg/24 h — to prevent adrenal crisis. For minor febrile illness, doubling the oral dose suffices. Every patient should carry an emergency injection kit.
items.reference: Treatment: "the need for immediate IV or IM injection of 100 mg hydrocortisone followed by intravenous infusion of 200 mg hydrocortisone/24 h in cases of prolonged vomiting, surgery, or trauma."

items.question: A young man with primary adrenal insufficiency has NEGATIVE adrenal (steroid) autoantibodies. Which test should be performed to identify a specific cause?
options: Very-long-chain fatty acids (to exclude X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy)
items.explanation: In males with antibody-negative primary adrenal insufficiency, very-long-chain fatty acids should be measured to exclude X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (ABCD1 mutation), in which VLCFAs accumulate. Antibody-positive patients have autoimmune adrenalitis; CT is used if antibodies are negative to look for hemorrhage/infiltration/masses.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Diagnosis: "In male patients with negative autoantibodies in the plasma, very-long-chain fatty acids should be measured to exclude X-ALD."

items.question: A child with chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis develops hypoparathyroidism and then adrenal insufficiency. Which syndrome and gene are responsible?
items.explanation: The triad of chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, hypoparathyroidism, and Addison's disease is APS1 (APECED), an autosomal-recessive disorder caused by AIRE mutations. APS2 is polygenic (HLA-DR3) and features autoimmune thyroid disease, vitiligo, and premature ovarian failure.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Etiology: "APS1... is caused by mutations in the autoimmune regulator gene AIRE... APS1 patients invariably develop chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis."

items.question: A patient has taken supraphysiologic prednisone for 6 months for an inflammatory disease and no longer needs it. How should it be discontinued?
options: Taper gradually toward a physiologic dose, then assess HPA recovery with an early-morning cortisol
items.explanation: Long-term (>3–4 weeks) supraphysiologic glucocorticoids suppress the HPA axis, so the drug is tapered gradually to a physiologic dose (~3–5 mg prednisone) and HPA recovery is then assessed (early-morning cortisol <150 nmol/L suggests GI-AI; >300 suggests recovery). Abrupt cessation risks adrenal crisis. Mineralocorticoid is not needed (aldosterone preserved).
items.reference: GI-AI: "patients on long-term treatment should be tapered down gradually until approaching a physiologic dose... an early-morning cortisol <150 nmol/L... is highly suggestive of GI-AI."

items.question: Which medication notably increases the risk of glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency by inhibiting CYP3A4?
items.explanation: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors raise systemic glucocorticoid exposure and the risk of HPA suppression; ritonavir (used in antivirals for HIV, hepatitis C, COVID-19) is the most frequently reported offender, along with azoles and certain antibiotics. Opioids can also blunt the HPA axis.
items.reference: GI-AI: "the concomitant administration of strong cytochrome P450 3A4 inhibitors... Ritonavir is the most reported offending medication."

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency accounts for 90–95% of congenital adrenal hyperplasia, and which metabolite is the diagnostic marker?
items.explanation: 21-Hydroxylase (CYP21A2) deficiency causes 90–95% of CAH; 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17OHP) accumulates proximal to the block and is the diagnostic marker (also used to monitor for overtreatment). The block impairs cortisol/aldosterone synthesis and shunts precursors into androgen excess.
items.reference: CAH: "Mutations in CYP21A2 are the most prevalent cause of CAH, responsible for 90–95% of cases... 17OHP is a useful marker."

items.question: A neonatal boy with classic 21-hydroxylase deficiency CAH is at risk of which life-threatening early presentation?
items.explanation: Classic (salt-wasting) CAH has combined glucocorticoid AND mineralocorticoid deficiency; boys (without virilized genitalia to prompt earlier diagnosis) typically present in the first weeks of life with a life-threatening salt-wasting adrenal crisis. Girls are often detected earlier because of virilized genitalia (46,XX DSD).
items.reference: CAH: "boys with classic CAH usually present with life-threatening adrenal crisis in the first few weeks of life (salt-wasting crisis)."

items.question: A young woman has hirsutism, oligomenorrhea, and mildly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone, with preserved cortisol production. Which diagnosis fits best?
items.explanation: Nonclassic CAH results from the mildest 21-hydroxylase mutations, presenting in adolescence/adulthood with a PCOS-like picture (hirsutism, oligomenorrhea) and preserved glucocorticoid production. Classic salt-wasting CAH presents in the neonatal period with combined deficiency.
items.reference: CAH: "The mildest mutations result in the least severe clinical phenotype, nonclassic CAH, usually presenting during adolescence and early adulthood... resembling a polycystic ovary syndrome phenotype."

items.question: A patient with hypopituitarism (panhypopituitarism) is unlikely to have a significant deficiency of which hormone?
items.explanation: Aldosterone is regulated by the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, not the pituitary, so hypothalamic-pituitary damage spares aldosterone synthesis. Cortisol, thyroxine, and growth hormone all depend on pituitary trophic hormones and can be deficient.
items.reference: Regulatory Control: "hypothalamic-pituitary damage does not significantly impact the capacity of the adrenal to synthesize aldosterone."

items.question: Which feature most suggests ECTOPIC ACTH syndrome rather than a pituitary cause of Cushing's?
options: Slow onset over years with classic cushingoid habitus
Rapid onset with marked hyperpigmentation, hypokalemia, edema, and very high ACTH
items.explanation: Ectopic ACTH syndrome (often occult carcinoids or small-cell lung cancer; more common in men) tends to present with rapid onset and prominent hyperpigmentation (excess POMC), hypokalemia, edema, and hypertension, with very high ACTH and resistance to dexamethasone suppression.
items.reference: Cushing's — Clinical: "In ectopic ACTH syndrome, hyperpigmentation... can be observed... patients with ectopic ACTH syndrome... may have a more rapid onset and progression of clinical signs and symptoms, namely edema, hypokalemia, and hypertension."

items.question: Why is a single random serum cortisol a poor test for adrenal insufficiency?
options: Cortisol is not measurable in serum
Cortisol has a diurnal rhythm, so a low value may simply reflect timing; stimulation testing reveals true reserve
items.explanation: Because cortisol secretion follows a strong diurnal rhythm, a random low value may just reflect the time of day, and some secondary-AI patients have near-normal basal cortisol but fail to respond to stimulation. The short cosyntropin test exposes inadequate cortisol reserve.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Diagnosis: "Random serum cortisol measurements are of limited diagnostic value because baseline cortisol levels may be coincidentally low due to the physiologic diurnal rhythm."

items.statement: Imaging (pituitary MRI or adrenal CT) should be performed before biochemically confirming hypercortisolism in suspected Cushing's syndrome.
items.explanation: False. Hypercortisolism must be confirmed biochemically and the ACTH-dependence determined BEFORE imaging, because pituitary and adrenal incidentalomas are common and imaging first leads to unnecessary surgery.
items.reference: Cushing's — Diagnosis: "imaging should only be used after it is established whether the cortisol excess is ACTH-dependent or ACTH-independent."

items.statement: In ACTH-independent (adrenal) Cushing's syndrome, plasma ACTH is suppressed.
items.explanation: True. Autonomous adrenal cortisol production suppresses pituitary ACTH via negative feedback, so ACTH is low — distinguishing it from ACTH-dependent disease (normal/high ACTH).
items.reference: Cushing's — Differential: "plasma ACTH levels are suppressed in cases of autonomous adrenal cortisol excess."

items.statement: A normal serum potassium excludes primary aldosteronism.
items.explanation: False. Only 10–40% of patients with primary aldosteronism are hypokalemic, so normokalemia does not exclude it; resistant hypertension alone is a screening indication.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Clinical: "only 10–40% of patients with primary aldosteronism exhibit hypokalemia."

items.statement: Unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma is treated medically with a mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist, while bilateral hyperplasia is treated surgically.
items.explanation: False — it is the reverse. Unilateral adenoma is treated with (potentially curative) adrenalectomy; bilateral hyperplasia is treated medically with an MR antagonist (spironolactone/eplerenone).
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Etiology: "unilateral forms are amenable to potentially curative surgery, whereas mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist therapy is the treatment of choice for bilateral forms."

items.statement: An adrenal mass with an attenuation <10 HU on unenhanced CT and normal hormonal workup can be considered benign without further imaging follow-up.
items.explanation: True. Such lipid-rich masses have a malignancy rate <0.5% and need no imaging follow-up. High-risk masses are >4 cm with >20 HU.
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "an attenuation value of <10 HU on unenhanced CT can be considered benign (malignancy rate <0.5%) and do not require further follow-up."

items.statement: FNA biopsy is the preferred test to distinguish a benign adrenocortical adenoma from an adrenocortical carcinoma.
items.explanation: False. FNA cannot reliably distinguish benign from malignant adrenocortical lesions, can violate an ACC capsule (needle-track metastasis), and can trigger a hypertensive crisis if the mass is a pheochromocytoma. It is reserved for suspected metastasis after excluding pheochromocytoma.
items.reference: ACC/Incidentaloma: "FNA is not indicated in suspected ACC... cytology and also histopathology of a core biopsy cannot differentiate between benign and malignant primary adrenal masses."

items.statement: Secondary (central) adrenal insufficiency typically causes both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid deficiency.
items.explanation: False. Secondary AI causes glucocorticoid deficiency only; aldosterone is regulated by the RAA system and is preserved. Primary AI (Addison's) loses both, producing hyponatremia and hyperkalemia.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Clinical: "In secondary adrenal insufficiency, only glucocorticoid deficiency is present, as the adrenal itself is intact and thus still amenable to regulation by the RAA system."

items.statement: In suspected adrenal crisis, treatment with IV fluids and hydrocortisone should begin immediately, without waiting for confirmatory test results.
items.explanation: True. It is reasonable to draw a baseline cortisol and then start saline and hydrocortisone immediately; formal stimulation testing can be deferred. Delaying treatment for testing risks death.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Diagnosis: "tests to establish the diagnosis of adrenal insufficiency should never delay treatment."

items.statement: Dexamethasone is the preferred glucocorticoid for routine daily replacement in chronic adrenal insufficiency.
items.explanation: False. Hydrocortisone (15–25 mg/day in divided doses) is preferred; long-acting steroids such as dexamethasone/prednisolone are avoided routinely because they over-expose tissues during the physiologic cortisol nadir.
items.reference: Treatment: "Long-acting glucocorticoids such as prednisolone or dexamethasone are not preferred because they result in increased glucocorticoid exposure."

items.statement: A course of supraphysiologic glucocorticoids lasting less than 3–4 weeks can generally be stopped abruptly.
items.explanation: True. Short courses (<3–4 weeks) are generally safe to stop abruptly; the highest risk of glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency is with supraphysiologic doses for longer than 3–4 weeks, which require gradual tapering.
items.reference: GI-AI: "If glucocorticoids are no longer required... and the treatment duration is <3–4 weeks, glucocorticoids can be stopped abruptly."

items.statement: In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, the enzyme block leads to androgen excess because precursors are shunted into androgen synthesis.
items.explanation: True. The block impairs cortisol synthesis, raising ACTH, which drives the adrenal; because the cortisol pathway is blocked, precursors are diverted into androgen synthesis, producing androgen excess.
items.reference: CAH: "This leads to increased pituitary ACTH release, which drives increased synthesis of adrenal androgen precursors and subsequent androgen excess."

items.statement: Excess licorice ingestion can cause hypokalemic hypertension by inhibiting 11β-HSD2.
items.explanation: True. Licorice inhibits 11β-HSD2, preventing inactivation of cortisol to cortisone in the kidney, so cortisol activates the mineralocorticoid receptor — producing hypokalemic hypertension with suppressed renin and aldosterone.
items.reference: Physiology: "Inhibition of 11β-HSD2 by excess licorice ingestion also results in hypokalemic hypertension."

items.statement: The Weiss score provides important prognostic information in adrenocortical carcinoma.
items.explanation: False. The Weiss score is used to DIAGNOSE ACC (≥3 elements) but carries no prognostic value; the Ki67 proliferation index is the key prognostic marker (≥10% = poor prognosis).
items.reference: ACC: "the Weiss score carries no prognostic value; the most important prognostic histopathologic parameter is the Ki67 proliferation index."

items.statement: Patients with established glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency require mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) replacement.
items.explanation: False. Glucocorticoid-induced (secondary) adrenal insufficiency preserves aldosterone production, so mineralocorticoid replacement is not required; patients need daily glucocorticoid plus stress-dose coverage.
items.reference: GI-AI: "Mineralocorticoid therapy is not required due to preserved aldosterone production."

items.assertion: Patients with secondary adrenal insufficiency usually do not require fludrocortisone.
items.reason: Aldosterone production is controlled by the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system and is preserved when the lesion is at the hypothalamic-pituitary level.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because aldosterone is RAA-regulated (not ACTH-dependent), pituitary/hypothalamic disease spares it, so mineralocorticoid replacement is not needed in secondary AI.
items.reference: Regulatory Control: "hypothalamic-pituitary damage does not significantly impact the capacity of the adrenal to synthesize aldosterone."

items.assertion: In confirmed Cushing's syndrome, plasma ACTH should be measured before adrenal or pituitary imaging.
items.reason: Pituitary and adrenal incidentalomas are common in the general population, so imaging first risks misdirecting management.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Determining ACTH-dependence first directs which gland to image and prevents being misled by common incidental nodules.
items.reference: Cushing's — Differential: "imaging should only be used after it is established whether the cortisol excess is ACTH-dependent or ACTH-independent because nodules... are a common finding."

items.assertion: Pheochromocytoma must be excluded before biopsy or surgery on an adrenal mass.
items.reason: Manipulating a pheochromocytoma can precipitate a life-threatening hypertensive crisis from catecholamine release.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Catecholamine surge during biopsy or surgery on an unrecognized pheochromocytoma can cause a fatal hypertensive crisis, so it is excluded first.
items.reference: Incidentaloma: "FNA of a pheochromocytoma can cause a life-threatening hypertensive crisis."

items.assertion: Serum potassium should be normalized before measuring the aldosterone-renin ratio.
items.reason: Hypokalemia suppresses aldosterone secretion, which can produce a false-negative aldosterone-renin ratio.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because low potassium suppresses aldosterone, testing while hypokalemic can falsely lower the ARR and miss the diagnosis; potassium is corrected first.
items.reference: Aldosteronism — Diagnosis: "serum potassium needs to be normalized prior to testing."

items.assertion: In the syndrome of apparent mineralocorticoid excess, cortisol causes hypokalemic hypertension.
items.reason: Loss of 11β-HSD2 activity allows cortisol to bind and activate the mineralocorticoid receptor.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Normally 11β-HSD2 inactivates cortisol in the kidney; without it, cortisol (present at ~1000× the concentration of aldosterone) activates the MR, causing mineralocorticoid-excess hypertension.
items.reference: Physiology: "only rapid inactivation of cortisol to cortisone by 11β-HSD2 prevents MR activation by excess cortisol."

items.assertion: Hyperpigmentation occurs in primary but not secondary adrenal insufficiency.
items.reason: Primary adrenal insufficiency is accompanied by elevated ACTH/POMC products that stimulate melanocytes.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Low cortisol in primary AI raises ACTH/POMC, stimulating melanocytes (hyperpigmentation); in secondary AI, ACTH is low, so the skin is pale.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Clinical: "hyperpigmentation, which is caused by excess ACTH stimulation of melanocytes... in secondary adrenal insufficiency, the skin has an alabaster-like paleness."

items.assertion: Patients on mitotane need a higher-than-usual glucocorticoid replacement dose.
items.reason: Mitotane induces hepatic CYP3A4, accelerating glucocorticoid inactivation, and increases cortisol-binding globulin.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Mitotane both blocks cortisol synthesis and (via CYP3A4 induction and raised CBG) accelerates glucocorticoid clearance and lowers free cortisol, so replacement is roughly doubled.
items.reference: ACC: "glucocorticoid replacement dose should be at least double... because mitotane induces hepatic CYP3A4 activity, resulting in rapid inactivation of glucocorticoids."

items.assertion: Glucocorticoid replacement in adrenal crisis should not be delayed for confirmatory testing.
items.reason: Adrenal crisis is rapidly life-threatening, and a baseline cortisol can be drawn before treatment with formal testing deferred.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because adrenal crisis can be fatal, treatment (saline + hydrocortisone) starts immediately after drawing a baseline cortisol, with stimulation testing performed later.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Diagnosis: "in a patient with suspected adrenal crisis, it is reasonable to draw baseline cortisol levels, provide replacement therapy, and defer formal stimulation testing."

items.assertion: Liddle's syndrome responds to amiloride but not to spironolactone.
items.reason: The defect is a constitutively active epithelial sodium channel rather than excess mineralocorticoid-receptor activation.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the problem is an overactive ENaC (not MR activation), an ENaC blocker (amiloride) works while an MR antagonist (spironolactone) does not.
items.reference: Aldosteronism: Liddle's "is very sensitive to amiloride treatment but will not respond to MR antagonist treatment because the defect is due to a constitutively active ENaC."

items.assertion: Glucocorticoid treatment in congenital adrenal hyperplasia aims not only to replace cortisol but also to suppress androgen excess.
items.reason: The elevated ACTH drive in CAH stimulates adrenal androgen precursor production, so adequate glucocorticoid lowers ACTH and reduces androgens.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. In CAH, low cortisol raises ACTH which drives androgen excess; glucocorticoid both replaces cortisol and suppresses the ACTH-driven androgen overproduction.
items.reference: CAH — Treatment: "it not only needed to replace missing glucocorticoids but also to control the increased ACTH drive and subsequent androgen excess."

items.assertion: Hyponatremia can occur in secondary adrenal insufficiency even though aldosterone is preserved.
items.reason: Cortisol normally inhibits ADH release, so cortisol deficiency leads to increased ADH and water retention.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Cortisol deficiency disinhibits ADH, producing an SIADH-like picture with hyponatremia — but without hyperkalemia, since aldosterone is intact.
items.reference: Adrenal Insufficiency — Clinical: "Hyponatremia... can also occur in secondary adrenal insufficiency due to diminished inhibition of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) release by cortisol."

items.assertion: Aldosterone production in glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism can be suppressed by dexamethasone.
items.reason: A chimeric gene places aldosterone synthase under the control of ACTH, which dexamethasone suppresses.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. In GRA, the CYP11B1/CYP11B2 chimeric gene puts aldosterone synthesis under ACTH control; suppressing ACTH with dexamethasone therefore lowers aldosterone.
items.reference: Aldosteronism: GRA "brings CYP11B2 transcription under the control of ACTH receptor signaling; consequently, aldosterone production is regulated by ACTH rather than by renin."

items.subtopic: Wiebke Arlt, Alessandro Prete | ADRENAL ANATOMY AND DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Understanding Adrenal Gland Anatomy and Blood Supply
items.content: The adrenal glands are essential for producing corticosteroids. They weigh 6–11 g each and have a distinct blood supply pattern that is crucial for their function.
keyPoints: Normal adrenal gland weight: 6–11 g each.
Location: above the kidneys with their own blood supply.
Blood flow path: subcapsular region, zona glomerulosa, zona fasciculata, zona reticularis, to the adrenal medulla.
Venous drainage: right adrenal vein into vena cava; left adrenal vein into left renal vein.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Wiebke Arlt, Alessandro Prete | ADRENAL ANATOMY AND DEVELOPMENT

items.subtopic: Wiebke Arlt, Alessandro Prete | ADRENAL ANATOMY AND DEVELOPMENT
items.title: Adrenal Development and Role of Nuclear Receptors
items.content: The development of the adrenal glands is closely linked to their function in steroidogenesis, regulated by specific nuclear receptors.
keyPoints: Origin: from urogenital ridge; separate from gonads and kidneys around sixth week of gestation.
Cortisol production starts during sexual differentiation (seventh to ninth week).
Key nuclear receptors: SF1 (NR5A1) and DAX1 (NR0B1), crucial for regulating adrenal genes involved in steroidogenesis.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Wiebke Arlt, Alessandro Prete | ADRENAL ANATOMY AND DEVELOPMENT

items.title: Understanding the HPA Axis Regulation of Glucocorticoid Synthesis
items.content: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis regulates glucocorticoid synthesis through a feedback mechanism. CRH from the hypothalamus stimulates ACTH release, which in turn regulates cortisol production by the adrenal glands.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids are regulated by the HPA axis via negative feedback involving CRH and ACTH.
ACTH is derived from POMC cleavage by PC1, resulting in a 39-amino acid peptide.
Cortisol secretion follows a circadian rhythm with peak levels in the morning.
Dexamethasone suppression test assesses cortisol excess; ineffective at lower doses for pituitary adenomas and resistant for ectopic ACTH sources.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · REGULATORY CONTROL OF STEROIDOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosing Glucocorticoid Deficiency with ACTH Stimulation
items.content: ACTH stimulation tests, using cosyntropin, assess adrenal function by evaluating cortisol response. A normal response is a cortisol level >15–20 µg/dL (400–550 nmol/L) 30–60 minutes post-stimulation.
keyPoints: ACTH peptide's first 24 amino acids are sufficient for physiological response.
Cosyntropin test involves administering 250 µg IM or IV and measuring cortisol at specified intervals.
Normal cortisol response is >15–20 µg/dL (400–550 nmol/L) post-cosyntropin administration.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · REGULATORY CONTROL OF STEROIDOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Insulin Tolerance Test for Assessing Adrenal Function
items.content: The insulin tolerance test evaluates adrenal function by inducing hypoglycemia to stimulate the HPA axis. A normal response includes cortisol >20 µg/dL and GH >5.1 µg/L.
keyPoints: Insulin tolerance test involves 0.1 U/kg IV insulin, with blood samples collected at multiple time points.
Normal response is defined by specific cortisol and growth hormone levels post-hypoglycemia induction.
Contraindications include coronary disease, cerebrovascular disease, or seizure disorders.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · REGULATORY CONTROL OF STEROIDOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding the RAA System's Role in Mineralocorticoid Regulation
items.content: The Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone (RAA) system regulates mineralocorticoid production, primarily aldosterone. Initiated by renin release from kidney cells, it leads to angiotensin II formation, which stimulates aldosterone synthesis via the AT1 receptor. This process increases sodium retention and potassium excretion, affecting blood volume and pressure.
keyPoints: Mineralocorticoid production is controlled by the RAA system.
Renin release from juxtaglomerular cells initiates the cycle.
Angiotensin II activates AT1 receptor to increase aldosterone production.
Aldosterone enhances renal sodium retention and potassium excretion.
RAA system regulation of mineralocorticoids is independent of hypothalamic-pituitary function.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · REGULATORY CONTROL OF STEROIDOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Implications of the RAA System in Mineralocorticoid Disorders
items.content: The RAA system's response to mineralocorticoid levels can aid diagnosis. In excess, plasma renin is downregulated; in deficiency, it is increased. Sodium loading tests reveal attenuated aldosterone suppression in autonomous mineralocorticoid excess.
keyPoints: Mineralocorticoid excess leads to decreased plasma renin levels.
Mineralocorticoid deficiency results in markedly increased plasma renin.
Sodium loading suppresses aldosterone, but not in cases of autonomous mineralocorticoid excess.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · REGULATORY CONTROL OF STEROIDOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 1)
items.title: ACTH and Steroidogenesis
items.content: Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) is essential for initiating steroidogenesis. It binds to the MC2R on adrenocortical cells, leading to cAMP production and PKA activation. This process increases cholesterol import into mitochondria, enhances hormone-sensitive lipase activity, and upregulates transcription factors like CREB, which boosts glucocorticoid synthesis.
keyPoints: ACTH binds to MC2R and requires MRAP for membrane transport.
cAMP production from ACTH stimulation activates PKA.
PKA activation increases cholesterol import, hormone-sensitive lipase activity, and transcription of CYP11A1.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 1)
items.title: Adrenal Steroidogenesis Pathways
items.content: Steroidogenesis in the adrenal cortex is zone-specific: mineralocorticoids are synthesized in the zona glomerulosa, glucocorticoids in the zona fasciculata, and androgens in the zona reticularis. Cholesterol import into mitochondria via StAR protein initiates these pathways, with various CYP enzymes facilitating specific steroid conversions.
keyPoints: Mineralocorticoid synthesis occurs in the zona glomerulosa.
Glucocorticoid synthesis takes place in the zona fasciculata.
Androgen biosynthesis is located in the zona reticularis.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 1)
items.title: Cortisol Metabolism and Action
items.content: Circulating cortisol is mainly bound to CBG and albumin. Free cortisol enters cells directly or is converted from cortisone by 11β-HSD1 in target tissues. Cortisol activates the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) leading to transactivation of glucocorticoid response elements or transrepression of proinflammatory genes, crucial for its anti-inflammatory effects.
keyPoints: Cortisol circulates bound to CBG and albumin.
11β-HSD1 converts cortisone to cortisol in target tissues using NADPH from H6PDH.
Glucocorticoid receptor activation by cortisol leads to transactivation or transrepression.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 2)
items.title: Cortisol Inactivation and Mineralocorticoid Receptor Modulation
items.content: Cortisol is rapidly inactivated to cortisone by the enzyme 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (11β-HSD2) primarily in the kidney, which prevents cortisol from activating mineralocorticoid receptors (MR). This mechanism is crucial because cortisol and aldosterone bind MR with equal affinity; however, due to cortisol's much higher circulating concentration (~1000-fold), rapid conversion to cortisone by 11β-HSD2 is essential for preventing inappropriate MR activation.
keyPoints: Cortisol is inactivated to cortisone by 11β-HSD2.
11β-HSD2 is mainly located in the kidney, colon, salivary glands, and other mineralocorticoid target tissues.
Cortisol circulates at about a 1000-fold higher concentration than aldosterone.
Rapid inactivation of cortisol to cortisone prevents MR activation by excess cortisol.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 2)
items.title: Mechanism of Aldosterone Synthesis and Action
items.content: Aldosterone synthesis in the adrenal zona glomerulosa is regulated by angiotensin II binding to the AT1 receptor.
