This interaction leads to membrane depolarization through inhibition of sodium-potassium (Na+/K+) ATPase enzymes and potassium channels, increasing intracellular calcium via voltage-dependent calcium channels or inhibition of calcium (Ca2+) ATPases. The resultant calcium signaling upregulates aldosterone synthase (CYP11B2) transcription. Aldosterone binds to the mineralocorticoid receptor in kidney tubule cells, dissociating the HSP-receptor complex and promoting dimerization and nuclear translocation. This enhances transcription of ENaC and SGK-1, facilitating sodium retention and potassium extrusion.
keyPoints: Aldosterone synthesis is driven by aldosterone synthase (CYP11B2).
Angiotensin II binding to AT1 receptor causes glomerulosa cell membrane depolarization.
Depolarization increases intracellular calcium, upregulating CYP11B2 transcription.
Aldosterone binds MR, leading to ENaC and SGK-1 transcription enhancement.
SGK-1 phosphorylates Nedd4, reducing its interaction with ENAC, promoting sodium retention.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · STEROID HORMONE BIOSYNTHESIS, METABOLISM, AND ACTION (part 2)

items.title: Epidemiology and Prevalence of Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: Iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome is the most common form, affecting around 1% of the population due to chronic glucocorticoid use. Endogenous Cushing's syndrome is rare with an incidence of 1.8–3.2 per million annually.
keyPoints: Iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome affects ~1% of the population.
Endogenous Cushing's syndrome has an incidence of 1.8–3.2 per million per year.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Etiology and Gender Distribution in Cushing's Disease
items.content: Most endogenous Cushing's syndrome cases are due to ACTH-producing pituitary adenomas. Cushing's disease more frequently affects women, except prepubertal cases where it is more common in boys.
keyPoints: 90% of Cushing's disease cases involve a corticotrope pituitary microadenoma.
Cushing's disease predominantly affects women, except in prepubertal cases.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Mutations and Ectopic ACTH Production
items.content: USP8 mutations are found in 11–62% of corticotrope adenomas. Ectopic ACTH production is often caused by small carcinoid tumors, particularly in the lung.
keyPoints: USP8 mutations occur in 11–62% of corticotrope adenomas.
Ectopic ACTH production is commonly due to occult carcinoid tumors, especially in the lung.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Primary Bilateral Macronodular Adrenal Hyperplasia in Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: Primary bilateral macronodular adrenal hyperplasia (PBMAH) is a rare cause of adrenal cortisol excess characterized by low circulating ACTH but intra-adrenal ACTH production. This condition involves aberrant expression of G protein-coupled receptors, leading to upregulation of PKA signaling and increased cortisol production.
keyPoints: PBMAH can be caused by germline and somatic mutations in ARMC5 gene.
Mutations in PRKACA and KDM1A are also associated with PBMAH.
GIP-dependent Cushing's syndrome is linked to KDM1A variants.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: Glucocorticoids affect multiple physiological systems, leading to signs such as upregulation of gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, and protein catabolism. Excess glucocorticoid secretion can cause hypertension, hypokalemia, edema, suppression of gonadotropins, and decreased TSH secretion.
keyPoints: Clinical features include obesity, diabetes, diastolic hypertension, hirsutism, depression, thinning skin, easy bruising, purplish striae, and proximal myopathy.
Distinct signs like hyperpigmentation in ectopic ACTH syndrome are due to excess ACTH effects on melanocytes.
Patients may experience deep vein thrombosis, psychiatric symptoms, and long-term risks of cardiovascular disease and osteoporosis.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Considerations for Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: Diagnosis should be considered when multiple clinical features are present, especially specific ones or those manifesting at an unusual age. Features like osteoporosis in young patients warrant suspicion of Cushing's.
keyPoints: Nonspecific symptoms include obesity, diabetes, and hypertension.
Specific signs such as thinning skin, purplish striae, and proximal myopathy are more indicative.
Ectopic ACTH syndrome may present with rapid onset of edema, hypokalemia, and hypertension.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 2)

items.title: Establishing a Diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: The correct diagnosis of Cushing's syndrome is essential to avoid unnecessary procedures. A diagnosis can be considered established if multiple tests consistently suggest Cushing's, including increased 24-h urinary free cortisol excretion in three separate collections, failure to suppress morning serum cortisol after overnight dexamethasone exposure, and loss of diurnal cortisol secretion with high midnight levels.
keyPoints: Exclude exogenous glucocorticoid use before testing.

Multiple tests should be consistently suggestive: 24-h urinary free cortisol in three collections, failure to suppress morning serum cortisol after overnight dexamethasone, high midnight serum or salivary cortisol.
Consider repeating tests if results are affected by factors such as incomplete urine collection or CYP3A4-inducing drugs.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 3)

items.title: Differentiating ACTH-Dependent and ACTH-Independent Cortisol Excess
items.content: Differential diagnosis of Cushing's involves determining whether cortisol excess is ACTH-dependent or independent. Plasma ACTH levels are suppressed in autonomous adrenal cortisol excess, while they are normal or increased in ACTH-dependent cases. Imaging should follow this determination to avoid unnecessary procedures.
keyPoints: Plasma ACTH levels help differentiate: suppressed in ACTH-independent; normal/increased in ACTH-dependent.
Imaging follows determination of ACTH dependency.
Unenhanced CT for adrenal imaging assesses morphology and tumor density (in Hounsfield units).
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 3)

items.title: Confirming Pituitary Cushing's Disease vs. Ectopic ACTH Syndrome
items.content: For confirmed ACTH-dependent cortisol excess, MRI of the pituitary is preferred but may miss small tumors. Further tests include high-dose glucocorticoid suppression and responsiveness to CRH or desmopressin for pituitary Cushing's disease. Ectopic sources typically resist dexamethasone suppression and are unresponsive to these agents.
keyPoints: MRI is first-line for ACTH-dependent cases but may miss small tumors.
Pituitary adenomas respond to high-dose glucocorticoids, CRH, or desmopressin; ectopic sources do not.
Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) with ACTH ratio >2 at baseline and >3 post-CRH indicates Cushing's disease.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 3)

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 4) | Ectopic ACTH Syndrome Imaging
items.title: Imaging Strategies for Ectopic ACTH Syndrome
items.content: When ectopic ACTH syndrome is suspected, high-resolution CT scans of the chest and abdomen are recommended to identify potential sources in the lung, thymus, or pancreas. If no lesions are found, MRI can be considered due to carcinoid tumors' characteristic T2 signal intensity. Additionally, octreotide scintigraphy and ^68Ga DOTATATE PET/CT may aid detection as these tumors often express somatostatin receptors.
keyPoints: High-resolution CT of chest and abdomen for ectopic ACTH syndrome
MRI if no lesions found due to carcinoid tumor characteristics on T2-weighted images
Octreotide scintigraphy and ^68Ga DOTATATE PET/CT can be helpful due to somatostatin receptor expression
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 4) | Cushing's Syndrome

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 4) | Treatment of Cushing's Disease
items.title: Treatment Approaches for Cushing's Disease
items.content: The primary treatment for Cushing's disease is the selective removal of the pituitary corticotrope tumor via an endoscopic transsphenoidal approach, with a cure rate of 60–80% by experienced surgeons. Remission rates drop to 12–70% in cases involving larger or invasive tumors. Post-surgery follow-up is crucial due to potential late relapse. If surgery fails or recurrence occurs, options include second surgery, radiotherapy, pharmacologic therapy, and bilateral adrenalectomy.
keyPoints: Endoscopic transsphenoidal approach for pituitary corticotrope tumor removal
Initial cure rate of 60–80% with experienced surgeons
Remission rates lower (12–70%) in larger or invasive tumors
Long-term follow-up necessary due to risk of late relapse
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 4) | Cushing's Syndrome

items.subtopic: CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 4) | Post-Treatment HPA Axis Recovery
items.title: HPA Axis Recovery After Tumor Removal in Cushing's Syndrome
items.content: Following the removal of an ACTH- or cortisol-producing tumor, hydrocortisone replacement is initiated to manage suppressed HPA axis function. Recovery rates vary: ectopic ACTH syndrome has the highest recovery rate (80%) with a median time of 7 months; adrenal Cushing's has the lowest (40%) with a median time of 30 months; Cushing's disease falls in between at 60% with a median time of 17 months.
keyPoints: Hydrocortisone replacement initiated post-tumor removal
Ectopic ACTH syndrome recovery rate: 80%, median time 7 months
Adrenal Cushing's recovery rate: 40%, median time 30 months
Cushing's disease recovery rate: 60%, median time 17 months
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CUSHING'S SYNDROME (part 4) | Cushing's Syndrome

items.title: Prevalence and Screening of Primary Aldosteronism
items.content: Primary aldosteronism is more prevalent than previously thought, with studies showing a prevalence ranging from 5 to 12% in hypertensive patients. This prevalence increases in those preselected for hypokalemic or drug-resistant hypertension.
keyPoints: Prevalence of primary aldosteronism ranges from 5 to 12% among all hypertensive patients.
Higher prevalence noted in patients with hypokalemic hypertension or drug-resistant hypertension.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · PRIMARY MINERALO CORTICOID EXCESS (part 1)

items.title: Etiology and Clinical Management of Primary Aldosteronism
items.content: Primary aldosteronism is primarily caused by excess aldosterone production, with bilateral micronodular adrenal hyperplasia being the most common form. Unilateral adenomas are amenable to surgery, whereas bilateral forms require mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists.
keyPoints: Bilateral primary aldosteronism due to adrenal hyperplasia is the most common form.
Unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn's syndrome) can be treated with surgery.
Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist therapy is used for bilateral forms.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · PRIMARY MINERALO CORTICOID EXCESS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic and Rare Causes of Primary Aldosteronism
items.content: Genetic mutations, such as those in KCNJ5, can cause familial primary aldosteronism. Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism results from a chimeric gene affecting ACTH regulation of aldosterone production.
keyPoints: Somatic and germline KCNJ5 mutations are linked to familial primary aldosteronism.
Glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism is caused by a chimeric gene between CYP11B1 and CYP11B2.
Recognition of genetic forms is crucial for early-onset hypertension management.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · PRIMARY MINERALO CORTICOID EXCESS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Mineralocorticoid Excess
items.content: Excess mineralocorticoids lead to potassium depletion and sodium retention, causing hypokalemic hypertension. This can result in metabolic alkalosis, muscle weakness, arrhythmias, and increased cardiovascular risks due to direct myocardial damage.
keyPoints: Hypokalemia is exacerbated by thiazide drugs.
Severe hypokalemia may cause polyuria, glucose intolerance, muscle weakness, rhabdomyolysis, or hypokalemic paralysis.
Metabolic alkalosis can lead to muscle cramps and tetany.
10-40% of primary aldosteronism patients exhibit hypokalemia.
Increased rates of osteoporosis, type 2 diabetes, and cognitive dysfunction are noted in these patients.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · PRIMARY MINERALO CORTICOID EXCESS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Screening for Mineralocorticoid Excess
items.content: Screening is recommended for hypertensive patients with drug resistance, hypokalemia, adrenal mass, early onset, or family history. The aldosterone-renin ratio (ARR) is used after normalizing serum potassium and stopping MR antagonists.
keyPoints: Positive ARR screening result: >750 pmol/L per ng/mL per hour.
Lower ARR cutoffs may be used to detect milder forms.
Antihypertensive medications like beta-blockers and ACE/AT1R inhibitors can affect ARR results.
Serum potassium must be normalized before testing.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · PRIMARY MINERALO CORTICOID EXCESS (part 2)

items.title: Confirmatory Testing for Mineralocorticoid Excess
items.content: The saline infusion test is a straightforward confirmatory test, with aldosterone suppression thresholds set at <170 pmol/L seated or <140 pmol/L recumbent. Alternative tests include oral sodium loading and fludrocortisone suppression.
keyPoints: Saline infusion test: IV 2 L saline over 4 hours; failure to suppress aldosterone indicates excess.
Oral sodium loading: 300 mmol NaCl/day for 3 days.
Fludrocortisone suppression test: 0.1 mg q6h with 30 mmol NaCl q8h for 4 days, risk of hypokalemia and hypertension.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · PRIMARY MINERALO CORTICOID EXCESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Clinical suspicion of mineralocorticoid excess Patients with hypertension and
items.title: Indications for Adrenal Venous Sampling (AVS) in Primary Aldosteronism
items.content: AVS is indicated to differentiate unilateral from bilateral forms of primary aldosteronism, especially when CT findings are inconclusive or a unilateral lesion is present in patients over 35 years. AVS involves measuring aldosterone and cortisol levels to confirm lateralization.
keyPoints: Severe hypertension (>3 BP drugs) or hypokalemia suggests mineralocorticoid excess.
AVS requires a cortisol gradient >2–3 between the vena cava and each adrenal vein.
Lateralization is confirmed by an aldosterone/cortisol ratio at least twofold higher on one side.
PET-CT with [14]C] metomidate can be a noninvasive alternative to AVS.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Clinical suspicion of mineralocorticoid excess Patients with hypertension and

items.subtopic: Clinical suspicion of mineralocorticoid excess Patients with hypertension and
items.title: Management Strategies for Primary Aldosteronism
items.content: Patients under 35 with confirmed primary aldosteronism and a unilateral lesion can proceed directly to surgery. Non-surgical candidates or those with bilateral hyperplasia should be treated medically, primarily using MR antagonists like spironolactone.
keyPoints: Surgery is indicated for patients <35 years with confirmed lateralization by AVS or PET-CT.
Spironolactone doses range from 12.5–50 mg daily, up to 400 mg/d.
Eplerenone can be used as an alternative MR antagonist, starting at 25 mg bid.
Amiloride (5–10 mg bid) is a useful potassium-sparing diuretic.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Clinical suspicion of mineralocorticoid excess Patients with hypertension and

items.subtopic: Clinical suspicion of mineralocorticoid excess Patients with hypertension and
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Non-Aldosterone-Related Mineralocorticoid Excess
items.content: Non-aldosterone-related mineralocorticoid excess is diagnosed by suppressed renin and aldosterone levels in the presence of hypokalemic hypertension. Urinary steroid metabolite profiling via GC/MS can identify specific enzyme deficiencies.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves suppressed renin and aldosterone with hypokalemic hypertension.
GC/MS detects steroids associated with CYP11B1/CYP17A1 deficiency or DOC-producing ACC.
Increased free cortisol over free cortisone ratio suggests non-aldosterone-related excess.
Liddle's syndrome, sensitive to amiloride, should be considered if GC/MS is normal.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Clinical suspicion of mineralocorticoid excess Patients with hypertension and

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 1)
items.title: Prevalence and Etiology of Adrenal Incidentalomas
items.content: Adrenal incidentalomas are common findings, with a prevalence of 2–5% in the general population. The likelihood increases with age, affecting about 1% of 40-year-olds and 7% of 70-year-olds. Most solitary adrenal tumors are monoclonal neoplasms, and several genetic syndromes can present with adrenocortical or pheochromocytomas.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 2–5% in the general population; increases to 1% at age 40 and 7% at age 70.

Common etiologies include monoclonal neoplasms and genetic syndromes such as MEN 1, RET, Carney's complex, McCune-Albright, Li Fraumeni, Lynch syndrome, familial adenomatous polyposis (associated with adrenocortical tumors), and VHL, MEN 2, paraganglioma syndromes, neurofibromatosis type 1, FH (associated with pheochromocytomas).
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 1)
items.title: Diagnostic Evaluation of Adrenal Masses
items.content: Adrenal masses larger than 1 cm necessitate diagnostic evaluation to determine if they secrete hormones or are benign/malignant. Key evaluations include ruling out catecholamine excess and primary aldosteronism, with cortisol excess being the most common hormonal abnormality observed.
keyPoints: Evaluate adrenal masses >1 cm for hormone secretion and malignancy risk.
Catecholamine excess from pheochromocytoma must be excluded; rule out primary aldosteronism in hypertensive patients.
Cortisol excess is most common, with MACS defined by early-morning cortisol >50 nmol/L after dexamethasone suppression.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 1)
items.title: Imaging and Differentiation of Adrenal Masses
items.content: Unenhanced CT is preferred for imaging adrenal glands, with tumor size and attenuation values aiding in differentiation. Benign lesions are typically rounded and homogenous, while malignant ones appear lobulated and inhomogeneous. MRI is useful without ionizing radiation exposure.
keyPoints: Use unenhanced CT as the primary imaging modality; consider MRI for children, young adults, and pregnant patients.
Size >4 cm has 80% specificity but poor predictive value alone for malignancy.
Adrenocortical adenomas often have low attenuation (<20 HUs), while ACCs and pheochromocytomas show high attenuation (>20 HU).
Benign lesions are rounded/homogenous; malignant ones are lobulated/inhomogeneous.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 1)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)
items.title: Evaluating FDG-PET for Adrenal Masses
items.content: FDG-PET can help differentiate between benign and malignant adrenal masses. Lack of FDG uptake typically indicates a benign mass, but specificity is limited as some malignancies like adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) are FDG avid, and certain metastases do not take up FDG.
keyPoints: Lack of FDG uptake usually suggests a benign adrenal mass.
Adrenocortical carcinoma is invariably FDG avid; specificity for malignancy is poor due to some benign adenomas also taking up FDG.
Caution needed in patients with extra-adrenal cancer history, as metastases (e.g., from renal cell carcinoma) may not take up FDG.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)
items.title: Management of Adrenal Masses Based on Size and CT Attenuation
items.content: Adrenal masses are assessed for malignancy risk based on size and unenhanced CT attenuation values. Small, low-attenuation masses (<4 cm, <10 HU) are likely benign with a malignancy rate <0.5%, while larger or higher-attenuation masses (>4 cm, >20 HU) have a 50% risk of malignancy and require swift attention.
keyPoints: Adrenal masses <4 cm and <10 HU on unenhanced CT are considered benign with malignancy rate <0.5%.
Masses <4 cm and 10–20 HU or >4 cm and 10–20 HU have a low malignancy risk (<5%) but need interval imaging.
>4 cm masses with attenuation >20 HU have a 50% risk of malignancy, necessitating prompt evaluation.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)

items.title: Genetic Mutations in ACC
items.content: Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) is associated with specific genetic mutations, which can influence its occurrence and behavior. Somatic TP53 mutations are present in a quarter of sporadic cases, while germline mutations are linked to syndromes like Li-Fraumeni and Lynch syndrome.
keyPoints: Somatic TP53 mutations found in 25% of sporadic ACC
Germline TP53 mutations cause Li-Fraumeni syndrome associated with ACC
TP53 mutation R337H prevalent in pediatric ACC cases in Brazil
Other genetic changes include Lynch syndrome, Carney complex (PRKAR1A), MEN1, familial adenomatous polyposis (APC), Wnt/β-catenin pathway alterations, and IGF2 overexpression found in 90% of ACCs
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENOCORTICAL CARCINOMA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Imaging in ACC
items.content: Diagnosing ACC involves distinguishing between benign and malignant adrenal masses, which is challenging due to histopathologic similarities. Imaging techniques like CT, MRI, and PET scans are crucial for staging and detecting metastases.
keyPoints: FNA not recommended for suspected ACC due to risk of needle canal metastasis
Weiss score used in histopathology; presence of three or more elements suggests malignancy
60-70% of ACCs show biochemical evidence of steroid overproduction, but often clinically asymptomatic
CT and MRI are informative for tumor origin determination; 18-FDG-PET is sensitive for detecting small metastases
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENOCORTICAL CARCINOMA (part 1)

items.title: Prognostic Factors and Treatment of ACC
items.content: The prognosis of ACC is significantly influenced by the Ki67 proliferation index, with higher values indicating poor outcomes. Complete surgical removal remains the only curative treatment, emphasizing early detection.
keyPoints: Ki67 <10% indicates slow to moderate growth; ≥10% associated with poor prognosis
Cure achieved through early detection and complete surgical removal
Capsule violation during surgery, metastasis at diagnosis, and nonspecialist treatment are linked to poor survival
En bloc removal of kidney and spleen may be necessary if tumor invades adjacent organs
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENOCORTICAL CARCINOMA (part 1)

items.title: Adjuvant Mitotane Therapy for High-Risk ACC
items.content: Mitotane is recommended as adjuvant therapy in patients with confirmed ACC who have a high risk of recurrence, specifically those classified under ENSAT stage III and IV or with a Ki67 proliferation index ≥10%. Treatment should be continued for at least 2 years if side effects are manageable.
keyPoints: Adjuvant mitotane indicated for ENSAT stage III/IV ACC or Ki67 ≥10%
Continue adjuvant mitotane for at least 2 years
Therapeutic plasma mitotane levels: 14–20 mg/L
Neurotoxic complications more frequent at >20 mg/L
Initial dose of mitotane: 500 mg tid, titrated up to 2000 mg tid
Maintenance doses range from 1000 to 1500 mg tid
Glucocorticoid replacement required due to disruption of cortisol synthesis; dose should be double the usual (40–60 mg daily in divided doses)
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENOCORTICAL CARCINOMA (part 2)

items.title: Management Strategies for ACC with Metastasis
items.content: For patients with metastatic ACC, surgical removal or radiofrequency ablation of single metastases is appropriate. If the tumor progresses during mitotane treatment, cytotoxic chemotherapy should be considered, using a regimen that includes cisplatin, etoposide, and doxorubicin along with continued mitotane. Painful bone metastasis can be managed with irradiation.
keyPoints: Surgical or radiofrequency ablation for single metastases
Cytotoxic chemotherapy if tumor progresses during mitotane: cisplatin, etoposide, doxorubicin plus mitotane
Irradiation for painful bone metastasis
Overall survival in ACC is poor with 5-year survival rates of 30–40%
Median survival in metastatic ACC is 15 months
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENOCORTICAL CARCINOMA (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence and Etiology of Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: Adrenal insufficiency is relatively rare, with a prevalence of 5 in 10,000. Hypothalamic-pituitary origin accounts for 3 in 10,000 cases, while primary adrenal insufficiency occurs at 2 in 10,000. Autoimmune destruction and genetic causes are significant contributors to primary adrenal insufficiency.
keyPoints: Prevalence of well-documented, permanent adrenal insufficiency is 5 in 10,000.
Hypothalamic-pituitary origin: 3 in 10,000; Primary adrenal insufficiency: 2 in 10,000.
Primary adrenal insufficiency often due to autoimmune adrenalitis (30-40%) or as part of APSs (60-70%).
APS1 is autosomal recessive, caused by AIRE gene mutations.
X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy incidence: 1 in 20,000 males.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 1)

items.title: Autoimmune Polyglandular Syndromes and Genetic Causes
items.content: APS1, or APECED, affects 10% of APS patients and involves mutations in the AIRE gene. APS2 has polygenic inheritance with associations to HLA-DR3 and other genes like CTLA-4. X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy results from ABCD1 gene mutations.
keyPoints: APS1 (APECED) affects 10% of APS patients, autosomal recessive, AIRE gene mutation.
APS2 has polygenic inheritance; associated with HLA-DR3 and CTLA-4 genes.
X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy: ABCD1 gene mutations cause accumulation of very-long-chain fatty acids.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: Primary adrenal insufficiency involves loss of both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid secretion, while secondary adrenal insufficiency affects only glucocorticoids. Hypothalamic-pituitary disease can cause additional endocrine issues or visual impairment.
keyPoints: Primary adrenal insufficiency: loss of glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid secretion.
Secondary adrenal insufficiency: only glucocorticoid deficiency, adrenal intact.
Hypothalamic-pituitary disease may cause additional endocrine issues or visual impairment.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Hyperpigmentation and Hyponatremia in Primary Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: Primary adrenal insufficiency is characterized by hyperpigmentation due to excess ACTH stimulation of melanocytes, most pronounced in areas exposed to friction or sunlight. Additionally, hyponatremia occurs in 80% of patients at presentation.
keyPoints: Hyperpigmentation is a distinguishing feature of primary adrenal insufficiency.
Hyponatremia found in 80% of patients with primary adrenal insufficiency.
Hyponatremia results from mineralocorticoid deficiency.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 2)

items.title: Identifying and Managing Acute Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: Acute adrenal insufficiency, or adrenal crisis, can present with symptoms mimicking acute abdomen or neurologic disease. It is more common in primary adrenal insufficiency due to loss of both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid secretion.
keyPoints: Adrenal crises occur at a rate of 8.3 per 100 patient-years, with 0.5 crisis-related deaths.
Symptoms can mimic acute abdomen or neurologic disease.
Triggers include intercurrent illness, surgical stress, or increased glucocorticoid inactivation.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: The short cosyntropin test is used for diagnosing adrenal insufficiency, with a cortisol level cutoff of <450–500 nmol/L after ACTH stimulation. Diagnosis should not delay treatment; baseline cortisol levels can be drawn before initiating therapy.
keyPoints: Short cosyntropin test is the diagnostic tool for adrenal insufficiency.
Cortisol level cutoff for failure: <450–500 nmol/L (16–18 µg/dL) after ACTH stimulation.
Diagnosis should not delay treatment; initiate therapy if crisis suspected.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 2)

items.title: Immediate Management of Acute Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: In cases of acute adrenal insufficiency, rapid initiation of treatment is critical. This involves rehydration with saline infusion at 1 L/h and glucocorticoid replacement starting with a bolus injection of 100 mg hydrocortisone followed by 200 mg over 24 hours.
keyPoints: Rehydrate using saline infusion at initial rates of 1 L/h with continuous cardiac monitoring.
Initiate glucocorticoid replacement with a bolus injection of 100 mg hydrocortisone, followed by administration of 200 mg hydrocortisone over 24 hours.
Mineralocorticoid replacement can be started once daily hydrocortisone dose is reduced to <50 mg.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Acute Adrenal Insufficiency

items.title: Glucocorticoid Replacement in Chronic Adrenal Insufficiency
items.content: Chronic adrenal insufficiency requires glucocorticoid replacement at a dose that mimics physiological cortisol production, typically 15-25 mg hydrocortisone per day. Dose adjustments may be necessary during pregnancy and stress.
keyPoints: Administer 15–25 mg hydrocortisone in two to three divided doses daily for chronic adrenal insufficiency.
Increase hydrocortisone dose by 50% during the last trimester of pregnancy.
At least one-half of the daily glucocorticoid dose should be administered in the morning upon awakening.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Acute Adrenal Insufficiency

items.title: Monitoring and Adjustments for Glucocorticoid Replacement
items.content: Monitor patients on glucocorticoid replacement by assessing signs of over- or underreplacement, including body weight and blood pressure. Supraphysiologic doses (>30 mg hydrocortisone) require regular bone density evaluations.
keyPoints: Assess for signs of glucocorticoid over- or underreplacement through history and physical examination.
Plasma ACTH, 24-h urinary free cortisol, or serum cortisol day curves are not reliable indicators of replacement quality.
Patients on supraphysiologic doses (≥30 mg hydrocortisone) should undergo regular bone mineral density evaluation.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Acute Adrenal Insufficiency

items.title: Risk Factors and Incidence of GI-AI in Glucocorticoid Users
items.content: Glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency (GI-AI) can occur with any route of exogenous glucocorticoid administration. The risk is influenced by factors such as the duration and dose of therapy, mode of administration, concomitant medications affecting glucocorticoid metabolism, and individual susceptibility.
keyPoints: Up to 0.5–1.8% of long-term oral glucocorticoid users develop GI-AI.
Patients treated with supraphysiologic doses for >3–4 weeks have the highest risk.
Short-term therapy is generally safe.
Around 8% of inhaled glucocorticoid users may develop GI-AI, increasing to 21–27% with high doses and treatment duration >1 year.
Intra-articular steroid injections have a reported incidence of ~50%, but evidence is limited.
Concomitant use of strong cytochrome P450 3A4 inhibitors (e.g., ritonavir) significantly increases the risk.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · GLUCOCORTICOID-INDUCED ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.title: Management and Monitoring of GI-AI During Glucocorticoid Tapering
items.content: When tapering glucocorticoids, patients on long-term treatment should be gradually reduced to a physiologic dose. Patients may experience withdrawal symptoms during tapering, which can sometimes necessitate a temporary increase in dose followed by a slower taper. Testing for HPA axis recovery is crucial once a physiologic dose is achieved.
keyPoints: Long-term glucocorticoid users should be tapered gradually to 3–5 mg of prednisone.
Withdrawal symptoms during tapering can include malaise, fatigue, nausea, muscle and joint pain, and mood disturbances.
An early-morning serum cortisol <150 nmol/L (or 5 μg/dL) suggests GI-AI; >300 nmol/L (or 10 μg/dL) indicates HPA axis recovery.
Cosyntropin stimulation test may be considered for indeterminate cortisol values.
Patients should be educated and monitored for adrenal insufficiency symptoms during tapering.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · GLUCOCORTICOID-INDUCED ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY

items.title: Understanding the Genetic Basis and Clinical Presentation of CAH
items.content: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is primarily caused by mutations in genes encoding steroidogenic enzymes, with CYP21A2 being responsible for 90–95% of cases. The condition leads to glucocorticoid deficiency and varying degrees of mineralocorticoid or sex steroid production depending on the enzymatic block. Clinical presentation varies from severe virilization in neonatal girls to hirsutism and oligomenorrhea in women with nonclassic CAH.
keyPoints: CYP21A2 mutations cause 90–95% of CAH cases.
Patients exhibit glucocorticoid deficiency; mineralocorticoids or sex steroids may be affected based on the enzymatic block.
Clinical presentation ranges from severe virilization in neonatal girls to hirsutism and oligomenorrhea in nonclassic CAH.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CONGENITAL ADRENAL HYPERPLASIA

items.title: Management Challenges of Glucocorticoid Treatment in CAH
items.content: Glucocorticoid treatment for CAH is complex due to the need to replace missing glucocorticoids and control increased ACTH drive. The lack of preparations mimicking diurnal cortisol secretion leads to prolonged ACTH stimulation and androgen production. In childhood, treatment aims at optimizing growth and preventing adrenal crisis, while in adults, it focuses on preserving fertility and minimizing side effects like metabolic syndrome.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoid treatment must address both glucocorticoid replacement and control of increased ACTH drive.
Current treatments do not mimic diurnal cortisol secretion, leading to prolonged ACTH stimulation.
Childhood treatment focuses on growth optimization and adrenal crisis prevention; adult treatment aims at preserving fertility and minimizing side effects.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · CONGENITAL ADRENAL HYPERPLASIA

items.title: Optimal Glucocorticoid Therapy in CAH
items.content: In Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH), hydrocortisone is preferred for preventing adrenal crisis, with dosages tailored to mimic normal cortisol production. For children, the dose ranges from 10–13 mg/m² per day, while adults may require intermediate-acting glucocorticoids like prednisone if hydrocortisone is insufficient.
keyPoints: Hydrocortisone dosage for children: 1–1.5 times normal cortisol production rate (~10–13 mg/m² per day).
Use of modified and delayed-release hydrocortisone can lower daily doses while maintaining control over steroid precursor excess.
Stress-dose glucocorticoids should be double or triple the usual dose during surgery, acute illness, or severe trauma.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia

items.title: Monitoring and Managing Mineralocorticoid Needs in CAH
items.content: Mineralocorticoid requirements in CAH are higher in children due to relative resistance, which decreases with kidney maturation. Regular monitoring of plasma renin is crucial to ensure it remains within the upper half of the normal range.
keyPoints: Higher mineralocorticoid needs in children due to relative resistance; reassessment needed in young adults.
Plasma renin should be regularly monitored and maintained within the upper half of the normal reference range.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia

Isolated hypokalemia with normal blood pressure

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following statements about FDG-PET is correct in the context of incidentally discovered adrenal masses?
options: FDG uptake reliably indicates a malignant adrenocortical mass.
A lack of FDG uptake reliably indicates a benign adrenocortical mass.
Adrenal metastases from renal cell carcinoma typically show high FDG uptake.
Specificity for detecting adrenocortical carcinoma using FDG-PET is high.
items.explanation: A lack of FDG uptake reliably indicates a benign adrenocortical mass, although care must be taken with adrenal metastases from cancers like renal cell carcinoma that may not take up FDG.

items.question: When is fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of an adrenal mass considered?
options: In all patients with incidentally discovered adrenal masses.
Only in patients with a history of nonadrenal malignancy and after excluding pheochromocytoma.
For any adrenal mass larger than 4 cm.
When imaging shows attenuation values >20 HU.
items.explanation: FNA should only be considered in a patient with a history of nonadrenal malignancy and after careful exclusion of pheochromocytoma, if the outcome will influence therapeutic management.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)

items.subtopic: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)
items.question: What is a key consideration for adrenal masses with normal endocrine biochemistry and an attenuation value of <10 HU on unenhanced CT?
options: They are considered malignant with high risk.
They can be considered benign and do not require further follow-up.
They should undergo fine-needle aspiration for diagnosis.
items.explanation: Adrenal masses of any size with normal endocrine biochemistry at diagnosis and an attenuation value of <10 HU on unenhanced CT can be considered benign (malignancy rate <0.5%) and do not require further follow-up.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT: INCIDENTALLY DISCOVERED ADRENAL MASS (part 2)

items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of primary adrenal insufficiency cases are acquired?

items.question: Which condition is a rare cause of adrenal insufficiency due to genetic mutations affecting adrenal development?

items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 1)

items.question: Which feature is most characteristic of primary adrenal insufficiency?

items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 2)

items.question: What test is considered safe and reliable for diagnosing adrenal insufficiency?
items.explanation: The short cosyntropin test is a safe and reliable tool for diagnosing adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex · ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (part 2)

Only after plasma ACTH levels are measured

Use of multiple glucocorticoid formulations simultaneously

Continue the same dose indefinitely to prevent withdrawal symptoms.
Gradually taper down until approaching a physiologic dose.
Increase the dose temporarily before stopping completely.

items.question: Which cortisol level in early-morning serum measurement is highly suggestive of HPA axis recovery?
